For a school fleet of 180 devices, a statistical number of units will fail — predictable warranty coverage beats unpredictable repair costs.

question.text: Thin clients are always a cheaper option than desktop PCs for schools because they have a lower purchase price.
question.explanation: False. While thin clients have a low per-unit purchase price ($200–$400 vs $600–$1,200 for a desktop), they require robust server infrastructure to run applications. A Windows Server Remote Desktop Services deployment, adequate network bandwidth to every classroom, and server hardware costs must be factored in. At small scale, the server infrastructure cost can easily exceed the device savings — making thin clients cost-effective only above a certain fleet size with existing server infrastructure.

question.text: Explain why standardising on one hardware model across a school's 180-device fleet is advantageous for IT management. Identify three specific benefits.
question.explanation: (1) Simplified imaging — one Windows OS image with all drivers and software can be deployed to every device. With different models, each requires its own driver set, multiplying imaging complexity. (2) Shared spare parts — keeping stock of one RAM type, one battery model, one AC adaptor, and one common replacement screen covers all 180 devices. With multiple models, spare part inventory multiplies. (3) Consistent support and troubleshooting — IT staff become expert in one hardware platform; faults are familiar and quicker to resolve. Training needs are reduced. Also possible: unified warranty management with one vendor contact.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · SPARKY IS CHECKING YOUR WORK
blocks.text:
Before I present the procurement plan to the Schools Cooperative:

1. You've recommended desktops for the senior secondary design labs and laptops for primary. An IT coordinator argues 'Laptops are better for senior secondary too — students can use them at home for assignments.' What's your counter-argument based on workload and TCO?
2. The cooperative's finance team asks 'Why can't we just buy gaming laptops from JB Hi-Fi? They have better specs for the same price.' Give three specific reasons why consumer gaming hardware may not be appropriate for a school fleet deployment.
3. The current fleet being replaced includes 180 PCs from 2018. You've been asked to handle disposal. What legal and ethical obligations apply to e-waste disposal in Western Australia, and name a certified disposal pathway?

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #3005 · BUNBURY HARDWARE INTEGRATORS
blocks.text: This is your Year 12 capstone maintenance callout. Client: Bunbury Regional Hospital IT Department. Three situations have escalated to us simultaneously. (1) The hospital's radiology server has been running 24/7 for 4 years without maintenance — it's now showing SMART errors on two drives. (2) A power outage last night caused an unprotected server to shut down ungracefully — it won't boot and the event logs show filesystem corruption. (3) The hospital wants a preventative maintenance schedule for all 12 servers in the server room. This is a clinical environment — uptime and data integrity are non-negotiable.
Preventative Maintenance: Scheduled, Not Reactive

Reactive maintenance — fixing things after they break — is expensive and risky. In a hospital environment, a server failure is a patient safety issue. Preventative maintenance is a structured programme to catch problems before they cause failures. It costs less, loses less data, and maintains uptime.

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · JAXEN 'SPARKY' MILLER, BUNBURY HARDWARE INTEGRATORS
blocks.text: SMART errors on a drive are the drive telling you it's about to die. Don't ignore them hoping they go away — they won't. The moment SMART shows reallocated sectors climbing, you initiate a controlled replacement during a scheduled maintenance window. If you wait until it fails completely, you're doing an emergency recovery with patients waiting. Respect the warnings.
Server Preventative Maintenance Programme

rows: Backup verification — confirm last backup completed and is restorable
Veeam Backup, Windows Server Backup, cloud backup console
SMART monitoring — check drive health status on all servers
Paessler PRTG, Zabbix, Windows Server event logs
Windows Server Updates — test and approve pending patches
Physical inspection — dust filter cleaning, cable checks, fan operation
Compressed air, visual inspection, thermal camera
Temperature and airflow check — verify server room HVAC operating in range
UPS battery test — verify runtime and replace batteries as needed
UPS management software (APC PowerChute), manual test
Full hardware audit — check all components, firmware versions, drive health
Hardware inventory tool, manufacturer diagnostic utilities

blocks.text: UPS: Uninterruptible Power Supply

A UPS is a battery-backed power device that sits between the mains power and the server. When mains power fails, the UPS immediately switches to battery — providing time for a graceful server shutdown or waiting for a generator to start. The hospital's server filesystem corruption came directly from an unexpected power loss without UPS protection.

rows: How long the UPS can run connected equipment on battery (minutes)
Enough time for automated graceful shutdown if power doesn't return
Capacity — higher VA/W supports more equipment
Must be rated above total connected load — typically 1.2× safety margin
Tells server to shut down gracefully when battery reaches critical level
APC PowerChute, Eaton Intelligent Power Manager — OS integration
Protects connected equipment from voltage spikes
Power surges cause component damage — all servers should have surge protection
Corrects voltage sags and swells before reaching equipment
Dirty power (fluctuating voltage) causes premature hardware failure
Replace batteries without powering down the UPS
Critical for 24/7 environments — zero downtime battery replacement

blocks.text: SMART Drive Monitoring and RAID

SMART (Self-Monitoring, Analysis and Reporting Technology) is built into every modern hard drive and SSD. It tracks hundreds of parameters — reallocated sectors, spin retry count, uncorrectable errors, temperature — and reports them to the OS. Rising SMART error counts are a reliable early warning of imminent drive failure.

rows: Data split across drives for speed — no redundancy
NOT for production servers — performance only
OS drives, small servers — simple protection
Like RAID 5 with an extra parity block
High-importance data where 2 simultaneous failures are possible
Mirrored pairs, then striped for speed

blocks.text: Disaster Recovery and Business Continuity

Disaster recovery (DR) is the plan for restoring systems after a major failure. Business continuity (BC) is the broader plan for maintaining operations while the recovery happens. In a hospital context, both are mandated by healthcare regulations. The key metrics are RTO (Recovery Time Objective — how long can you be down?) and RPO (Recovery Point Objective — how much data can you afford to lose?).

rows: Maximum tolerable downtime before the system must be restored
Radiology server: RTO = 2 hours (patients can't wait longer for scans)
Maximum tolerable data loss — how old can a restored backup be?
Patient records: RPO = 15 minutes (must not lose more than 15 min of records)
How often backups run — must be more frequent than the RPO
RPO = 15 min → continuous replication or backups every 10 minutes
A copy of data stored physically or logically separate from primary location
Replication to a second data centre or Azure Backup
A secondary location that can run critical systems if the primary fails
A hot standby server in a different building or cloud region

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · HOSPITAL SERVER ROOM — THREE SITUATIONS
blocks.text:
Working through Ticket #3005 (radiology server SMART errors, post-outage filesystem corruption, maintenance schedule):

Situation 1 — Radiology server SMART errors:
1. The SMART report shows 'Reallocated Sectors: 47' and climbing on drive 2 of a 4-drive RAID 5 array. Walk through your exact response — what do you do right now, in what order?
2. Can you replace the failing drive while the server stays online? Explain what RAID feature enables this and what the risk is during the rebuild.

Situation 2 — Post-outage filesystem corruption:
3. The server shows 'CHKDSK found errors' and the filesystem is partially inaccessible. List your recovery steps in order. At what point would you declare data loss and initiate a backup restore?
4. What UPS specification should the hospital have had in place to prevent this? Calculate the minimum VA rating if the server draws 800W and the hospital needs 20 minutes of battery runtime.

Situation 3 — Maintenance schedule:
5. Create a 12-month preventative maintenance calendar for the hospital's 12 servers. Include quarterly, monthly, and annual tasks. Format as a table.
6. The hospital's IT manager asks: 'Should we replace all 12 servers now or one at a time over 3 years?' Give Sparky's recommendation with a specific rationale.

items.definition: A battery-backed power device that provides continuous power during mains failure, allowing graceful server shutdown or generator startup.
Built-in drive diagnostic system that tracks health parameters and warns of impending failure. Monitored by tools like CrystalDiskInfo, PRTG, or server management consoles.
Combines multiple drives for performance and/or redundancy. RAID is not a backup — it protects against drive failure only, not against deletion, corruption, or ransomware.
The maximum acceptable downtime after a system failure. Defines how quickly a system must be restored.
The maximum acceptable data loss after a failure. Defines how frequently data must be backed up.
The ability to replace a component (drive, power supply) while the system continues running without downtime.

question.text: A server's SMART report shows 'Reallocated Sectors Count: 250 (threshold: 5)' — far above the alert threshold. What does this indicate and what is the correct response?
options: The drive is performing well — higher numbers in SMART are always better
The drive is at risk of imminent failure; initiate a controlled replacement during a scheduled maintenance window immediately
Run chkdsk to repair the sectors and the drive will return to normal operation
The SMART report is inaccurate for SSDs — ignore it and monitor performance instead
question.explanation: Reallocated sectors means the drive has detected bad sectors and remapped data to spare space. When this count significantly exceeds the threshold and is rising, the drive is signalling imminent failure. The correct response is immediate controlled replacement: procure a replacement drive, verify backup is current, replace the failing drive, allow the RAID array to rebuild, then monitor. Do not wait for the drive to fail completely.

question.text: A RAID 5 array provides a complete backup solution — if any drive fails, all data can be recovered with no data loss, eliminating the need for separate backups.
question.explanation: False. RAID 5 provides protection against a single drive failure — not a backup. RAID does not protect against: (1) accidental file deletion, (2) ransomware encrypting all data on the array simultaneously, (3) a second drive failing during RAID rebuild (very common — rebuild stress causes latent faults to surface), (4) theft or physical destruction of the server. Separate backups are always required in addition to RAID.

question.text: Explain the difference between RTO and RPO, and describe a scenario where a hospital might have a very low RPO but a relatively higher RTO tolerable for a specific system.
question.explanation: RTO (Recovery Time Objective) is the maximum acceptable downtime — how long the system can be offline after a failure. RPO (Recovery Point Objective) is the maximum acceptable data loss — how old the restored data can be. Example scenario: A hospital's appointment booking system might have an RPO of 1 hour (losing an hour of appointment data is inconvenient but manageable — staff can call patients to rebook) but a relatively higher RTO of 8 hours (the system can be down overnight while IT restores it, as bookings are paused rather than lost). Contrast with the patient records system which might have RPO of 5 minutes and RTO of 30 minutes — data loss or extended downtime there is a direct clinical safety risk.

question.text: A hospital server room had a power outage last night. The server suffered filesystem corruption and won't boot. What hardware would have PREVENTED this outcome?
options: Additional RAM — more memory reduces the risk of data corruption on shutdown
A RAID 1 mirror — identical data on a second drive would survive the outage
A UPS (Uninterruptible Power Supply) with automatic graceful shutdown software configured
An NVMe SSD — flash storage is more resistant to sudden power loss than HDDs
question.explanation: A UPS provides battery power during mains failure, giving the server time to perform a graceful shutdown (properly closing all files and flushing the filesystem cache). Without a UPS, an unexpected power cut leaves the filesystem in a partially written state — causing corruption. RAID protects against drive failure, not power loss. Additional RAM and SSD type do not prevent graceful shutdown from occurring.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · SPARKY IS CHECKING YOUR WORK
blocks.text:
Final sign-off for your Year 12 Hardware rotation. Three capstone questions:

1. The hospital's IT manager says 'We have RAID 6 on all drives, so we don't need offsite backups — two drives can fail and we're fine.' Write Sparky's 3-point response covering scenarios RAID 6 cannot protect against.
2. During the RAID 5 rebuild after replacing the failing radiology drive, a second drive in the array shows a SMART uncorrectable read error and the RAID controller reports degraded status. What has happened, what data risk does this create, and what do you do next?
3. Looking back across all five Year 12 Hardware lessons — systems/components, OS, software compatibility, procurement, and maintenance — if you had to name the single skill that separates a competent junior tech from a senior engineer, what would it be and why?

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #3001 · REGIONAL ETHICS TECH GROUP
blocks.text: INTERN BRIEFING: A regional arts organisation has asked the Ethics Tech Group to review their digital content library. They've been using images from Google Image Search in their promotional materials, streaming a third-party soundtrack on their website, and sharing digitised copies of published books for a reading program — without licences for any of it. Before their annual grant report is filed, they need a full IP compliance audit. Your analysis needs to reference the Copyright Act 1968.

Intellectual property law exists to reward creators by giving them exclusive rights over how their work is used and distributed. In the digital age, those rights are constantly tested by the ease of copying, remixing, and sharing content. Understanding the Copyright Act 1968 is not optional for IT professionals — every website, app, and digital publication you produce is a potential copyright minefield.

items.definition: The exclusive legal right of a creator to reproduce, publish, communicate, and adapt their original work — automatically applied in Australia under the Copyright Act 1968 from the moment the work is created, with no registration required.
A broad category of legal rights over creations of the mind — including copyright, patents, trademarks, and trade secrets. In digital contexts, copyright is the most frequently relevant.

A set of specific, narrowly defined exceptions in the Copyright Act 1968 that permit limited use of copyrighted material without permission — limited to research, criticism, review, news reporting, parody, and judicial proceedings.

Non-economic rights of creators under the Copyright Act 1968 that cannot be sold or transferred: the right of attribution (to be named as author), the right against false attribution, and the right of integrity (protection against derogatory treatment of their work).

A licensing framework that allows copyright holders to grant specific permissions for reuse in advance — ranging from free use with attribution (CC BY) to more restrictive terms. CC licences are widely used for open educational resources and stock media.

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · DR. ELARA FINCH, REGIONAL ETHICS TECH GROUP
blocks.text: The phrase 'it was free online so I assumed it was free to use' has ended careers and cost organisations tens of thousands of dollars in litigation. In Australia, copyright attaches automatically at creation — no watermark, no © symbol, no registration needed. If someone made it, they own it. Your job as an IT professional is to know that before you download, embed, or redistribute anything.
The Copyright Act 1968 — Core Framework

The Copyright Act 1968 (Cth) protects original literary, artistic, musical, and dramatic works, as well as published editions, sound recordings, films, broadcasts, and software code. Copyright protection lasts for the life of the author plus 70 years in Australia. There is no need to register — protection is automatic and immediate upon creation.

rows: Software code, web content, ebooks, reports, scripts
Logos, UI designs, photographs, illustrations, icons
Video content, explainer animations, streamed recordings

blocks.text: Fair Dealing Exceptions

Fair dealing in Australia is strictly purpose-based — unlike the broader US 'fair use' doctrine, Australian fair dealing only applies to a closed list of permitted purposes. It is not a blanket defence for any 'reasonable' use.

rows: Reproducing a reasonable portion of a work for personal academic research
Distributing copies to a whole class; commercial research
Quoting from a work to critique or comment on it
Reproducing large extracts; copying without commentary
Using brief clips or quotes to report current events
Reproducing full articles or broadcasts for convenience
Creating a comedic or satirical work based on the original
Simply copying with minor modifications and calling it a parody
Using material as evidence in court or legal processes
Using as a general exemption outside formal legal proceedings

blocks.text: Creative Commons Licences

rows: Use, share, adapt for any purpose including commercial
Attribution required; derivative works must carry same licence
Use and adapt for non-commercial purposes
Share but not adapt the original work
Use for any purpose, no attribution required

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · IP COMPLIANCE AUDIT
blocks.text:
Review the following digital content used by the arts organisation:
• 12 images sourced from Google Image Search — no licence checked
• A Spotify playlist embedded in the website's homepage
• PDF copies of 5 published novels distributed to 80 participants in a reading program
• A promotional video using a 30-second clip from a YouTube documentary

For each item: (1) identify the likely copyright status; (2) determine whether fair dealing applies; (3) recommend what the organisation must do to achieve compliance. Cite specific sections of the Copyright Act 1968 where relevant.
Moral Rights — The Non-Commercial Dimension

Even when an organisation has purchased or licenced content, moral rights remain with the original creator. Failing to attribute authorship, misrepresenting the source, or using a work in a context that distorts or demeans it can breach moral rights — even if copyright was legally cleared. This is especially relevant in digital publishing and social media content curation.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · DR. FINCH IS IN THE REVIEW
blocks.text:
Before the IP audit report is delivered:
1. The arts organisation argues that they only used small portions of the copyrighted novels — surely that falls under fair dealing for research? Is this correct? What factors determine whether a use qualifies as fair dealing?
2. A volunteer photographer claims the photos she took at the organisation's events belong to the organisation since they paid for her travel. Who owns the copyright in the photographs, and why?
3. The organisation wants to post a parody of a well-known song on social media. What does Australian law require for a parody defence to succeed under the Copyright Act 1968?

question.text: Under the Copyright Act 1968, when does copyright protection begin for an original work in Australia?
options: When the creator registers the work with the Australian Copyright Council
When the creator adds a © symbol to the work
Automatically when the original work is created and expressed in material form
After the work has been published commercially
question.explanation: In Australia, copyright protection attaches automatically at the moment an original work is expressed in material form (written, recorded, drawn, coded, etc.). No registration, no symbol, and no publication is required.

question.text: A student copies three chapters of a textbook to share with their study group of 15 people. Which of the following most accurately describes the legal position?
options: This is lawful because fair dealing permits copying for research or study
This is likely an infringement — fair dealing for research permits personal use but not distribution to a group
This is lawful because education is always exempt from copyright
This is lawful because textbooks are government publications
question.explanation: Fair dealing for research or study in Australia permits personal copying of a reasonable portion of a work. Distributing copies to a group of 15 people goes beyond what the Copyright Act 1968 permits under the research exception — this is likely infringement.

question.text: Explain the difference between economic rights and moral rights under the Copyright Act 1968. Give one example of each being breached in a digital publishing context.
question.explanation: Award 2 marks for correctly explaining that economic rights (reproduction, communication, adaptation rights) can be transferred or licenced, while moral rights (attribution, integrity, false attribution) remain permanently with the creator and cannot be transferred. Award 1 mark each for a valid example of each breach, e.g., reproducing an image without a licence (economic rights) and using a photographer's image in a political advertisement without their consent or attribution (moral rights — integrity).

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #3002 · REGIONAL ETHICS TECH GROUP
blocks.text: INTERN BRIEFING: The Ethics Tech Group produces policy reports that are published publicly online and cited by government bodies. Last month, an intern submitted a report that included unattributed text from three academic sources and used statistics from a government website without acknowledgement. The organisation's credibility is at stake. Your task is to audit the report and develop a referencing standard that all team members will follow going forward.

Referencing is not just an academic formality — it is a professional and legal obligation. In the IT and policy sectors, citing your sources accurately demonstrates intellectual integrity, protects you against plagiarism allegations, and allows readers to verify your claims. Digital publications have their own referencing conventions, and as an IT professional producing public-facing documents, you are expected to know them.

items.definition: Presenting someone else's work, ideas, or words as your own, without attribution — whether intentional or accidental. A breach of academic integrity and, in published professional contexts, a potential copyright violation.
A brief reference inserted within the body of a document to indicate the source of a specific piece of information, quotation, or idea — linking to the full reference in the bibliography.
The complete list of all sources cited in a document, formatted according to the referencing style in use (APA, Harvard, Chicago, etc.).
A widely used academic and professional referencing style developed by the American Psychological Association — commonly required in Australian education and public policy documents.

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · DR. ELARA FINCH, REGIONAL ETHICS TECH GROUP
blocks.text: I've seen careers damaged by unreferenced reports. Not because the ideas were wrong — often they were right — but because the lack of attribution made the author look dishonest. In policy work, your credibility is everything. A well-referenced report says: 'I've done the research, I can back every claim up, and I'm not hiding where my ideas come from.' That's the standard we hold ourselves to here.
Why Referencing Matters in IT Contexts

IT professionals regularly produce public-facing documents: technical reports, policy submissions, software documentation, user guides, and white papers. Each of these may draw on external research, statistics, legislation, or other people's designs. Acknowledging these sources is required by professional ethics, copyright law, and often by the commissioning organisation's own standards.
Referencing Digital Sources — Worked Examples

rows: Author, A. A. (Year, Month Day). Title of page. Site Name. URL
Author, A.A. Year, Title of page, Site Name, viewed Day Month Year, <URL>
Department Name. (Year). Title of report (Report No. X). Publisher. URL
Department Name Year, Title of report, Report No. X, Publisher, URL
Author, A. A. [Channel Name]. (Year, Month Day). Title of video [Video]. YouTube. URL
Author, A.A. Year, Title of video [video], YouTube, viewed Day Month Year, <URL>
Author, A. A. (Year, Month Day). Article title. Publication Name. URL
Author, A.A. Year, 'Article title', Publication Name, Day Month, URL

blocks.text: In-Text Citations for Digital Sources

rows: (Author, Year, p. X) or (Author, Year, para. X for webpages)

blocks.text: Plagiarism — Consequences in Professional IT Contexts

rows: Fail grade, academic misconduct investigation, suspension or exclusion
Retraction of the document, reputational damage, employment termination
Copyright infringement claim, financial liability, loss of client contract
Disqualification, investigation, professional body disciplinary action
Platform takedown, public naming and shaming, defamation counterclaim

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · REFERENCING AUDIT
blocks.text:
The following excerpt from the intern's report has three referencing problems. Identify each problem and rewrite the paragraph with correct APA 7th edition in-text citations. Then write the full reference list entries for the three sources.

'According to research, Australians spend an average of 5.5 hours per day on digital devices. The Australian Privacy Principles require organisations to notify affected individuals within 30 days of a data breach. As has been noted by many experts, the digital divide disproportionately affects rural and Indigenous communities in Australia.'

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · DR. FINCH IS IN THE REVIEW
blocks.text:
Before the referencing standard is published to the team:
1. A colleague says that paraphrasing someone's idea in your own words means you don't need to reference it. Is this correct? What are the ethical and legal implications?
2. The team frequently cites government websites that are updated and sometimes taken offline. What referencing practice minimises risk when citing web-based sources?
3. A team member has used an AI tool to help draft a section of the report. How should AI-generated content be acknowledged in a professional document — and is there a copyright issue with the AI's output?

question.text: In APA 7th edition, how is a direct quote from a webpage (with no page numbers) cited in-text?
options: (Author, Year)
(Author, Year, para. X) where X is the paragraph number
(Author, Year, n.p.)
No in-text citation needed — just include the URL in brackets
question.explanation: When quoting directly from a webpage that has no page numbers, APA 7th edition recommends using a paragraph number (para. X), section heading, or timestamp as a locator: (Author, Year, para. 3). This allows readers to find the specific passage.

question.text: An IT student copies three sentences from a government website into their project report without attribution. Identify (a) the academic integrity issue and (b) the possible copyright issue this creates.
question.explanation: Award 2 marks for identifying that this constitutes plagiarism — presenting another's words as one's own without attribution, breaching academic integrity policies. Award 2 marks for identifying the copyright dimension — government website content is typically protected by Crown copyright under the Copyright Act 1968 (unless explicitly licensed otherwise), and reproducing it without acknowledgement or permission may constitute copyright infringement.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #3003 · REGIONAL ETHICS TECH GROUP
blocks.text: INTERN BRIEFING: A mid-sized technology company has approached the Ethics Tech Group for urgent advice. One of their software engineers posted a thread on X (Twitter) criticising a client's product, using their work email in their bio. The client has seen the posts and is threatening to terminate the service contract. The engineer says it was their personal account and they have a right to free speech. Your briefing must cover: digital citizenship, workplace social media obligations, and the legal and reputational risks.

Social networking has created an unprecedented merging of professional and personal identity online. Digital citizenship in a professional context goes beyond avoiding cyberbullying — it encompasses how publicly visible behaviour reflects on employers, how platforms can amplify both positivity and harm at speed, and what specific legal obligations apply to online conduct in Australian workplaces.

items.definition: The responsible, ethical, and legally compliant use of digital platforms in professional contexts — including representing an employer's brand, respecting client confidentiality, and complying with workplace social media policies.

An organisational policy that defines acceptable and unacceptable use of social media by employees — particularly where the employee's online identity is connected (explicitly or implicitly) to their employer.
Legal doctrine where an employer may be held legally responsible for the online conduct of an employee if the conduct is sufficiently connected to their employment — even on a personal account.
Publishing a false statement of fact that damages a person's or organisation's reputation. Social media posts, reviews, and comments are all subject to defamation law in Australia.

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · DR. ELARA FINCH, REGIONAL ETHICS TECH GROUP
blocks.text: I've had to tell CEOs that one of their engineers' tweets just cost them a $2 million contract. 'It was a personal account' doesn't matter when the bio says the company name. Every IT professional is a walking brand ambassador the moment they put their employer in their profile. Social media policy isn't bureaucratic overreach — it's basic risk management that protects both the company and the individual.
Forms of Cyberbullying on Social Platforms

rows: Persistent abusive or threatening messages directed at an individual
Direct messages on Instagram; replies on X (Twitter)
Coordinated group attacks on a specific person's posts
Quote-tweeting with mockery; Facebook group targeting
Publishing private personal information (address, employer, family members)
Creating fake accounts using another person's name or image
Fake Instagram profiles; spoofed LinkedIn accounts
Coordinated negative reviews designed to damage someone's reputation or business
Sharing private conversations or DMs publicly to humiliate
Any platform — content shared to public stories or feeds

blocks.text: Workplace Social Media Obligations

In Australia, the Fair Work Commission has repeatedly upheld dismissals of employees for social media posts that damaged an employer's reputation, disclosed confidential information, or harassed colleagues — even on personal accounts. The key legal test is whether the post has a sufficient nexus (connection) to the employment relationship.

items: Never identify your employer in association with personal opinions on controversial topics
Never post confidential client information, project details, or internal communications
Never use social media to harass or demean colleagues, clients, or competitors
Review your employer's social media policy before posting anything that could be perceived as professional commentary
Remember that content posted 'privately' can be screenshotted and shared publicly without your consent

blocks.title: Spot the Cyberbullying / Policy Breach
blocks.instruction: Review these two social media scenarios. Identify which one constitutes a cyberbullying/policy breach, and explain the specific risks it creates for the individual and their employer.

emails.from: Employee X — Personal Twitter/X account, bio: 'Senior Dev @ TechCo | Opinions my own'
emails.subject: Public tweet thread
emails.body:
Thread: Why [Client Company]'s new product is a disaster 🧵

1/ Their UX is genuinely embarrassing. Our team has been telling them for 6 months. They don't listen.
2/ Here's the actual user testing data they ignored [attaches screenshot of internal report]
3/ Don't buy it until they sort out the security issues. Trust me, I know.
4/ If anyone from [Client Company] is reading — yes, this is public, and yes, I work on your account.

redFlags.detail: Naming the client publicly while working on their account breaches confidentiality obligations — a potential contract and employment termination risk
Attaching a screenshot of internal testing data likely breaches the NDA and confidentiality provisions of the service contract
'Opinions my own' disclaimers do not legally sever the connection when the bio names the employer — vicarious liability risk for TechCo
Making public claims about 'security issues' with a client's product could constitute defamation if the claims are false or exaggerated

emails.subject: Post
emails.body: Excited to be attending the Australian Tech Conference this week! Looking forward to sessions on AI ethics and data governance. Always learning — that's what keeps this industry interesting. #techconf #auspol #AI

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · SOCIAL MEDIA POLICY DRAFT
blocks.text: Using the scenario at TechCo as your case study, draft a 'Social Media and Digital Conduct Policy' for a technology company of 50 employees. Your policy must include: (1) scope — what accounts and platforms it covers; (2) specific prohibited conduct with examples; (3) obligations regarding client and company confidentiality online; (4) the consequences of breaching the policy, referencing Fair Work Act 2009 provisions; and (5) a process for reviewing borderline cases before posting. The policy must be clear enough that a new employee could follow it without legal training.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · DR. FINCH IS IN THE REVIEW
blocks.text:
Before the social media policy is finalised:
1. An employee argues that a policy restricting personal social media posts is an invasion of privacy. Under Australian employment law, what is the legal balance between an employer's right to protect its reputation and an employee's right to personal expression?
2. The policy prohibits 'negative commentary about clients.' Could this provision itself breach whistleblower protections if an employee is trying to report genuine misconduct at a client company?
3. How should the policy address AI-generated content posted by employees — for example, using ChatGPT to draft professional posts? What specific risks does AI-generated content introduce that human-authored posts don't?

question.text: An employee is dismissed for a social media post made on their personal account, outside work hours, that criticised a client. The employee argues this is unfair because it was personal. Under Australian law, which factor most determines whether the dismissal is lawful?
options: Whether the post was made outside of working hours
Whether the employer has a written social media policy
Whether there is a sufficient connection between the post and the employment relationship
Whether the post was made anonymously
question.explanation: Australian Fair Work Commission decisions consistently apply a 'nexus' test — the key question is whether there is a sufficient connection between the employee's online conduct and their employment. Factors include: identifying the employer, discussing clients or colleagues, and the impact on the employer's reputation. Time of posting is only one minor factor.

question.text: Describe two specific online behaviours that could constitute defamation under Australian law and explain what an individual or organisation would need to prove to succeed in a defamation claim.
question.explanation: Award 1 mark each for two valid examples (e.g., posting a false review claiming a business committed fraud; sharing fabricated quotes attributed to a named individual; publicly accusing someone of a crime without basis). Award 3 marks for correctly identifying the elements of defamation: (1) the statement was published (made available to at least one other person); (2) it identified the plaintiff; (3) it was defamatory (likely to lower them in the estimation of reasonable people or cause them to be shunned).

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #3004 · REGIONAL ETHICS TECH GROUP
blocks.text: INTERN BRIEFING: The Ethics Tech Group has been retained by a state government department to review its digital work policies following a spike in staff burnout reports. Staff are expected to respond to Slack messages after hours, take work calls on personal phones during leave, and remain 'available' 24/7 via email. The union has flagged this as a wellbeing and industrial relations risk. Your brief is to advise on what the research says, what the law permits, and what a healthy digital work policy looks like.

Digital technology was supposed to give us more flexibility. In many organisations, it has instead made us perpetually available. The erosion of the boundary between work and personal life — enabled by smartphones, remote access, and always-on communication platforms — has produced measurable increases in burnout, anxiety, and reduced productivity. As IT professionals and policy advisors, we have a responsibility to design and advocate for healthier digital work environments.

items.definition: The state of physical, psychological, and social health that results from a person's relationship with technology — including the ability to set boundaries around digital use and recover genuine rest time.

A legal right provided under the Fair Work Legislation Amendment (Closing Loopholes No. 2) Act 2024 (Cth), giving eligible Australian employees the right to refuse unreasonable employer contact outside working hours from 26 August 2024.
An organisational expectation that employees remain contactable and responsive outside standard working hours via digital tools — a driver of chronic stress and work-life boundary erosion.
A state of chronic physical and emotional exhaustion caused by prolonged stress, characterised by cynicism, reduced efficacy, and disengagement — recognised by the WHO as an occupational phenomenon.

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · DR. ELARA FINCH, REGIONAL ETHICS TECH GROUP
blocks.text: The organisations I worry about most are the ones where leadership wears their 24/7 availability as a badge of honour. They have normalised a culture where rest is seen as weakness and boundary-setting reads as a lack of commitment. The research is unambiguous: chronically overworked people make worse decisions, produce lower quality work, and leave organisations faster. A healthy digital boundary policy isn't soft — it's strategic.
How Technology Affects Personal and Professional Boundaries

rows: Inability to switch off; sleep disruption from late-night notifications
Expectation of immediate response; work conversations intrude into home time
Work intrudes into home environment; blurs when 'work' ends
Meeting fatigue; lack of informal social contact; 'always on camera' stress
Employer can effectively contact staff 24/7; personal device becomes a work obligation

blocks.text: The Right to Disconnect — Australian Law

Since 26 August 2024, eligible Australian employees have a legal right to refuse unreasonable contact from their employer outside of working hours under the Fair Work Legislation Amendment (Closing Loopholes No. 2) Act 2024. Factors determining whether contact is 'reasonable' include the reason for the contact, whether extra pay is provided for availability, and the employee's role and personal circumstances. This is a landmark change in Australian employment law.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · DIGITAL WELLBEING POLICY
blocks.text: Design a 'Digital Wellbeing Policy' for the government department. Your policy must address: (1) hours during which staff are expected to respond to digital communications; (2) how the Right to Disconnect (Fair Work Act) will be implemented; (3) standards for after-hours meetings, messages, and email; (4) manager expectations and modelling behaviour; and (5) what support is available for staff experiencing digital overwhelm. Include a rationale section explaining why this policy is in the organisation's strategic interest, not just a staff welfare issue.

blocks.title: Ethical Dilemma: The Always-On Manager
blocks.scenario: Priya manages a team of 8 IT support officers. She routinely sends Slack messages and emails at 10pm and on weekends, and responds to her own messages instantly at all hours. She doesn't explicitly require her team to respond outside hours, but team members report feeling pressure to respond because she can see when messages are read. One team member, Marcus, has asked HR if he can turn off Slack notifications after 6pm. Priya's manager has told her to 'tone it down' but she says she works when inspiration strikes and it shouldn't affect others.

questions.question: Under the Right to Disconnect (Fair Work Act 2024), does Marcus have the legal right to ignore Priya's after-hours messages? Are there limits to this right?
Even if Priya is not explicitly requiring responses, identify the specific psychological and cultural mechanism that is creating pressure on the team, and explain how this constitutes a WHS risk.
As an external adviser from the Ethics Tech Group, what specific recommendations would you make to Priya's manager to resolve this situation — covering both individual and systemic changes?

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · DR. FINCH IS IN THE REVIEW
blocks.text:
Before the digital wellbeing policy is finalised:
1. The department's CIO argues that IT staff are different from other employees — systems go down at 3am and someone has to respond. How should the policy handle genuinely on-call roles versus the general expectation of availability?
2. Research shows that banning smartphones at work improves focus but reduces flexibility. How would you advise the department to balance these competing interests in a policy context?
3. The policy will require manager behaviour change. What specific mechanisms (not just guidelines) would you build into the policy to ensure managers actually change, rather than just paying lip service to digital wellbeing?

question.text: Under the Fair Work Legislation Amendment (Closing Loopholes No. 2) Act 2024, from what date do eligible Australian employees have the right to refuse unreasonable contact outside working hours?
options: 1 January 2024
26 August 2024
1 July 2025
This right does not yet exist in Australian law
question.explanation: The right to disconnect came into effect for most employees on 26 August 2024 under the Fair Work Legislation Amendment (Closing Loopholes No. 2) Act 2024. It gives eligible employees the right to refuse unreasonable contact from their employer outside of working hours.

question.text: Explain what 'burnout' is, identify two ways that digital technology contributes to burnout in modern workplaces, and suggest one organisational strategy to reduce this risk.
question.explanation: Award 1 mark for a correct definition of burnout (chronic physical and emotional exhaustion from prolonged stress; WHO-recognised occupational phenomenon). Award 1 mark each for two valid technology-related causes (e.g., always-on communication expectations eroding rest; notification overload disrupting concentration; inability to detach from work via remote access). Award 1 mark for a valid organisational strategy (e.g., right to disconnect policy; meeting-free blocks; notification curfews; digital detox leave).

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #3005 · REGIONAL ETHICS TECH GROUP
blocks.text: INTERN BRIEFING: During a recent natural disaster, a social media post containing false evacuation instructions spread to 200,000 people within 45 minutes. Three families followed the incorrect route and were placed in danger. The Ethics Tech Group has been asked to brief the State Emergency Management Committee on: how social media enables this kind of rapid misinformation spread, what platforms are legally required to do, and what policies should govern official emergency communication. This is a public safety and information ethics issue.

Social media has fundamentally restructured how information moves through society. The gatekeeping function previously performed by journalists, editors, and broadcasters has been democratised — anyone can now publish to a potential global audience in seconds. This is simultaneously liberating and dangerous. As IT professionals and digital policy advisors, we need to understand both the architecture that makes this possible and the ethical, legal, and social implications that follow.

items.definition: False or inaccurate information spread without intent to deceive — often the result of sharing content without verifying its accuracy.
False information deliberately created and spread with the intent to deceive — including state-sponsored propaganda, fake news campaigns, and coordinated inauthentic behaviour.
The process by which social media algorithms preferentially distribute emotionally engaging content — including outrage, fear, and sensationalism — at the expense of accuracy or nuance.
The degree to which social media platforms are legally responsible for the content their users publish — an actively contested area of Australian and international law.

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · DR. ELARA FINCH, REGIONAL ETHICS TECH GROUP
blocks.text: Social media platforms are not neutral pipes — they are opinionated editorial systems that decide, via algorithm, what you see and how prominently. And their editorial choices are optimised for engagement, not truth. Content that makes you angry or afraid is more engaging than content that is accurate and balanced. That's not an accident; it's a business model. Understanding that is the first step to thinking clearly about information distribution in the digital age.
How Social Networking Changed Information Distribution

Prior to social media, information reached the public through a limited number of channels — broadcast television, radio, newspapers — each with editorial standards, regulatory obligations, and production delays that functioned as a quality filter. Social media removed all of these friction points simultaneously. The result is a system where the speed of information spread is inversely correlated with the time available for verification.

rows: Hours to days from event to publication
Seconds from event to global distribution
None by default — individual users publish without review
Limited by broadcast geography and subscription
Named journalists; legal responsibility for publishers
Corrections rarely reach the same audience as the original false claim
Editorial standards limit most extreme content
Algorithms amplify high-emotion content regardless of accuracy

blocks.text: Platform Responsibilities Under Australian Law

The Online Safety Act 2021 (Cth) places obligations on social media platforms to remove illegal and harmful content, including cyberbullying material and content that could cause serious harm. The Australian Communications and Media Authority (ACMA) has been given new powers under the ACMA Misinformation Bill to require platforms to take action against systemic misinformation. These regulatory frameworks are evolving rapidly.

blocks.title: Spot the Misinformation
blocks.instruction: Review these two social media posts circulating during an emergency event. Identify which is likely misinformation and explain the specific indicators and risks.

emails.subject: URGENT EVACUATION — shared 8,400 times in 2 hours
emails.body: BREAKING: Roads closed — ALL residents in the Shire of X must evacuate via Highway 7 IMMEDIATELY. Do NOT use Stirling Road — it is cut off by fire. Share this NOW. Lives depend on it. [No source link. No official agency logo.]

redFlags.detail: Account created 2 days ago with 47 followers — not a verified official emergency account. Legitimate emergency alerts come from @DFES_WA or @WAPoliceForum
No link to DFES, police, or local government source. Official emergency alerts always reference the issuing authority and provide a verification link
'Share NOW. Lives depend on it.' is a manipulation tactic common in misinformation — genuine emergency services do not rely on viral sharing for safety-critical communication
Directing people to a specific route without official verification could cause harm — if this is wrong, people following it may drive into danger

emails.subject: Emergency Alert — Shire of X
emails.body:
EMERGENCY ALERT — DFES UPDATE 14:32:
All residents east of Stirling Road in the Shire of X should prepare to evacuate. Monitor the Emergency WA website (emergency.wa.gov.au) and ABC Emergency (720AM) for route instructions. Do NOT rely on social media for evacuation routes. Call 000 if in immediate danger.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · EMERGENCY INFORMATION PROTOCOL
blocks.text: Using the misinformation incident as your case study, design an 'Emergency Information Protocol' for the State Emergency Management Committee. Your protocol must specify: (1) the authoritative channels for emergency information publication; (2) how platforms should be required to label or remove unverified emergency content; (3) what the public should be advised to do before sharing emergency information; and (4) what legal mechanisms currently exist — and what gaps remain — in Australian law for addressing emergency misinformation.

blocks.title: Ethical Dilemma: The Viral False Story
blocks.scenario: A journalist at a regional newspaper writes a factually accurate story about a local council's decision to close a community pool. A social media influencer with 180,000 followers reads the headline (but not the article) and posts: 'This is DISGUSTING — they're closing EVERY pool in the shire to save money while giving themselves pay rises!!!' The post is shared 12,000 times within 3 hours. The council receives death threats. The influencer refuses to delete the post, saying 'that's my interpretation and I have free speech.'

questions.question: Does the influencer's post constitute defamation of the council? What would the council need to prove to succeed in a defamation action?
What obligation, if any, does the social media platform have to act on this content — and under which Australian law?
The influencer claims 'free speech.' Does Australia have a constitutional right to freedom of speech that would protect this post? Explain your answer carefully.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · DR. FINCH IS IN THE REVIEW
blocks.text:
Before the emergency protocol is finalised:
1. The protocol proposes that platforms should be required to remove unverified emergency content within 30 minutes. What are the technical, legal, and human rights challenges with this requirement?
2. A platform argues that it has no editorial responsibility for user-generated content — it is merely a 'conduit.' How does Australian law respond to this argument, and has it changed since 2021?
3. Who bears moral responsibility when misinformation causes harm — the original poster, the people who shared it, the algorithm that amplified it, or the platform that hosts it? Construct a reasoned argument.

question.text: What is the key distinction between 'misinformation' and 'disinformation'?
options: Misinformation is only found on social media; disinformation appears in mainstream news
Misinformation is false content spread without intent to deceive; disinformation is false content deliberately created to deceive
Misinformation is less harmful than disinformation
There is no legal distinction between the two terms in Australian law
question.explanation: Misinformation refers to false or inaccurate content spread without deliberate deceptive intent — often because the sharer hasn't verified it. Disinformation is deliberately created and distributed to deceive. The intent is the key distinguishing factor, though the harm caused may be similar.

question.text: Analyse how social media algorithms contribute to the spread of misinformation. In your response, explain (a) how algorithmic amplification works; (b) why emotionally engaging false content spreads faster than accurate content; and (c) what responsibilities platforms, governments, and individuals each have in addressing this problem. (Approximately 250 words)
question.explanation: High-quality responses will explain that algorithms are designed to maximise engagement (time-on-platform), and that emotional content — particularly fear, outrage, and surprise — generates more engagement than neutral accurate content. False but emotionally compelling claims therefore propagate faster through algorithmic systems than corrections. Award marks for: accurate description of engagement-optimised algorithms; discussion of how this creates asymmetry between the speed of false claims and corrections; and a balanced discussion of platform obligations (Online Safety Act 2021, ACMA powers), government regulatory responsibilities, and individual media literacy obligations. 8-mark responses should demonstrate analytical depth, not just description.

lessons.title: File Formats for Graphics and Audio

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #3001 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 IN PROGRESS | Sprint 12 | Assigned to: Junior Developer

Leo's design assets for the Collie River Festival campaign have arrived in the wrong formats — again. We have a 47 MB BMP logo, a WAV voiceover at 96 kHz/24-bit, and a client who wants everything embedded in a webpage by Friday. Sparky's hardware display requires SVG for the LED signage controller. Sloane needs compressed CSVs, not images, for the analytics dashboard — but Leo keeps sending PNGs of tables.

Your sprint task: audit every asset in the /assets folder, produce a format conversion plan with justification for each decision, and execute the conversions using the approved toolchain. Document your choices in the sprint notes. We ship Friday. No excuses.
Why File Format Decisions Are Delivery Decisions

At Year 12 level, choosing a file format is not a technical checkbox — it is a project management decision. The wrong format wastes bandwidth, breaks rendering pipelines, and kills deadlines. When Leo sends a 47 MB BMP and the client's CMS has a 2 MB upload limit, that is not Leo's problem: it is a project failure that lands on your sprint board.

Every format choice involves three trade-offs: file size, quality fidelity, and compatibility. Lossy compression (JPEG, MP3) discards data permanently to reduce size — ideal for web delivery where pixel-perfect quality is unnecessary. Lossless compression (PNG, FLAC) preserves every bit but produces larger files suited to editing workflows and archiving. Understanding which trade-off is acceptable in context separates a junior who follows instructions from a mid-level who makes decisions.

Raster and vector graphics have fundamentally different use cases. Raster formats (JPEG, PNG, WebP) store pixels — they look perfect at the intended size and break apart when scaled up. Vector formats (SVG, AI) store mathematical paths — they scale infinitely with no quality loss, which is exactly what Sparky's LED controller needs. Always ask: will this asset be displayed at multiple sizes? If yes, vector is the right default unless the content is photographic.

items.definition: A compression method that permanently removes data to reduce file size, resulting in quality degradation. Used in JPEG and MP3 formats. Each re-save compounds the quality loss.
A compression method that reduces file size without discarding any data. The original file can be perfectly reconstructed. Used in PNG and FLAC formats.
An image stored as a grid of pixels. Quality degrades when scaled beyond the original resolution. Examples: JPEG, PNG, GIF, WebP, BMP.
An image stored as mathematical paths and shapes. Scales to any size without quality loss. Examples: SVG, AI, EPS.
The number of bits used to represent each audio sample. Higher bit depth (e.g. 24-bit vs 16-bit) allows greater dynamic range. Production uses 24-bit; distribution typically uses 16-bit.
The number of audio samples captured per second, measured in Hz or kHz. CD quality is 44.1 kHz; broadcast is 48 kHz. Higher rates increase file size without perceptible benefit to most listeners.

rows: Photographs, web images where quality loss is acceptable
Editing source files; images with text or sharp edges
Graphics with transparency, screenshots, images with text
Large photographic images needing small file sizes
Web delivery — smaller than JPEG/PNG at equivalent quality
Older browsers or non-web contexts without WebP support
Simple looping animations, graphics with flat colour
Photographs or any image requiring more than 256 colours
Logos, icons, charts — any graphic scaled at multiple sizes
Photographs or highly complex raster images
Almost all modern digital production contexts
Music and speech distribution, podcast delivery
Audio editing source files; situations where quality is critical
Audio editing and production master files
Web streaming or any context where file size matters
High-quality audio archiving and audiophile distribution
Streaming platforms, Apple ecosystem, web audio

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text: I had a junior once who converted every image to PNG 'because it's lossless.' The homepage took 14 seconds to load on a 4G connection. The client's bounce rate doubled. PNG is not better — it is appropriate in specific contexts. Master the question: what is this asset's final destination and who is the audience? A photographic hero image on a public website is a JPEG at 80% quality every single time. A logo used across merchandise, signage, and a website is an SVG every single time. Context drives format. Format drives delivery. Delivery drives outcomes.

blocks.title: Audio Format Trade-offs: WAV vs MP3 vs AAC vs FLAC

items.label: WAV (Production Master)
points: Uncompressed PCM audio — no quality loss whatsoever
File size: ~10 MB per minute at 44.1 kHz / 16-bit
At 96 kHz / 24-bit (Sparky's hardware spec): ~34 MB per minute
Use for: recording, editing, mixing, archiving
Never distribute to end users as WAV — too large

items.label: MP3 (Universal Distribution)
points: Lossy compression using perceptual coding (removes sounds humans typically do not notice)
320 kbps = near-CD quality; 128 kbps = acceptable for speech/podcasts
File size: ~1 MB per minute at 128 kbps
Universal compatibility across all devices and platforms
Re-encoding MP3 to MP3 compounds quality loss — always encode from WAV master

items.label: AAC (Modern Streaming)
points: Lossy compression with better efficiency than MP3 at equivalent bitrates
Preferred format for Apple platforms (iTunes, Apple Music, iOS)
~20% smaller than MP3 at equivalent perceptual quality
Use for: streaming services, mobile apps, YouTube uploads
Less universal than MP3 but improving rapidly

items.label: FLAC (Lossless Distribution)
points: Lossless compression — identical to WAV in quality, ~50–60% of the file size
Not supported natively in all browsers — check deployment environment
Used by audiophile platforms: Bandcamp, Tidal, Qobuz
Ideal when distributing to musicians or audio professionals
Not appropriate for general web use without fallback format

blocks.title: Format Audit and Conversion — Festival Campaign Assets
blocks.software: GIMP / Audacity / VS Code
blocks.intro: Work through the Collie River Festival asset set and execute the required format conversions. Document your decisions as you go — Rory will review your sprint notes.
steps: Open GIMP. Go to File > Open and load the provided BMP logo file (festival-logo.bmp).
Inspect the image: check Image > Canvas Size for dimensions and Image > Mode to confirm it is RGB. Note the file size displayed in the title bar.

Go to File > Export As. In the filename field, change the extension to .svg. GIMP does not natively export SVG paths — instead, export as PNG at maximum resolution (600 dpi) for web use. Name it festival-logo-web.png.

Separately, open the BMP in Inkscape (free vector editor). Use Path > Trace Bitmap (Shift+Alt+B) with 'Colours' mode to create a true vector SVG. Save as festival-logo.svg for Sparky's LED controller.
Open Audacity. Go to File > Import > Audio and load the WAV voiceover (voiceover-master.wav).
Check the track properties: note the sample rate (bottom-left of track) and bit depth. If it is 96 kHz / 24-bit, go to Tracks > Resample to set 44100 Hz, then Tracks > Mix and Render.
Go to File > Export > Export as MP3. Set quality to 192 kbps (good balance for spoken word delivery). Name it voiceover-web.mp3.
For a second export (archive quality), go to File > Export > Export as WAV at 44100 Hz / 16-bit. Name it voiceover-cd.wav.
In VS Code, create a new file sprint-notes.md. Document each conversion: original format, new format, justification (one sentence), and the tool used.
Cross-reference with the deployment checklist: confirm each web asset is under 2 MB and that the SVG file opens correctly in a browser tab before marking the ticket as Ready for Review.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · FORMAT DECISION MATRIX
blocks.text:
Sloane has just dropped a new requirement: the analytics dashboard needs embedded charts. She has given you three options for each chart and wants you to pick one with justification.

Chart A — Monthly visitor numbers (bar chart, 800×400 px, displayed at various sizes on the dashboard):
Option 1: PNG export from Excel  |  Option 2: SVG export from Google Sheets  |  Option 3: JPEG screenshot

Chart B — Festival atmosphere hero image (photograph, 3000×2000 px, displayed at 1200×800 px max):
Option 1: Original TIFF at 18 MB  |  Option 2: JPEG at 85% quality (~1.2 MB)  |  Option 3: WebP at equivalent quality (~800 KB)

Chart C — Animated ticket sale counter (simple 4-frame animation, flat colour):
Option 1: GIF  |  Option 2: Animated SVG  |  Option 3: MP4 video

For each chart, write: (a) your chosen format, (b) why it is optimal for this context, and (c) one real-world risk if you chose one of the rejected options.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS REVIEWING YOUR SPRINT
blocks.text:
Three questions before I merge this ticket:

1. You converted the WAV from 96 kHz to 44.1 kHz before exporting to MP3. What specific audio quality information, if any, is lost in that sample rate reduction — and is that loss perceptible to a typical festival website visitor?

2. The SVG you produced with Inkscape's Trace Bitmap tool has a file size of 340 KB — larger than the PNG equivalent. What does that tell you about the source image, and when would you choose PNG over SVG even for a logo?

3. Sloane's analytics pipeline ingests data in CSV format, but Leo keeps sending PNG screenshots of Excel tables. Beyond the obvious 'it is not machine-readable,' what are two other specific technical consequences of receiving data as a PNG image rather than a CSV?

question.text: Leo sends you a logo file in BMP format. The logo will be used on the website, on a 6-metre event banner, and on Sparky's LED display controller. Which format is most appropriate for all three uses?
options: PNG — lossless raster format that preserves quality
JPEG — small file size suitable for web delivery
SVG — vector format that scales to any size without quality loss
GIF — supports animation and has wide browser compatibility
question.explanation: SVG is a vector format that stores graphics as mathematical paths rather than pixels. This means it scales from a small website favicon to a 6-metre banner and to any LED resolution without quality loss. PNG and JPEG are raster formats that would become pixelated when scaled up. GIF is limited to 256 colours and is also a raster format.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #3002 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🔴 URGENT | Sprint 13 | Assigned to: Junior Developer

We have a compliance problem. A previous junior installed unlicensed copies of Adobe Premiere on three studio machines, used a cracked version of a font pack in client deliverables, and distributed modified open-source code without including the required attribution file. Legal has flagged all three issues. The studio is potentially liable.

Your sprint task: (1) audit all software on the studio machines and classify each licence type, (2) produce a licence compliance report for the studio manager, and (3) recommend cost-effective replacements for any non-compliant software. This is not optional. Compliance is part of delivery.
Software Licences: The Legal Framework You Cannot Ignore

Software licences define what you are legally permitted to do with a piece of software. When you install software, you are not purchasing the software itself — you are purchasing a right to use it under the terms the developer specifies. Violating those terms is not a technical mistake: it is a legal exposure that can result in fines, legal action, and reputational damage for the studio.

The landscape of software licencing has expanded significantly beyond the traditional commercial vs. free binary. Open-source licences form a spectrum from highly permissive (MIT, Apache 2.0) to strongly protective (GPL). Understanding the difference matters in a professional context: some open-source licences require you to release your own code if you incorporate theirs. That is a business decision, not just a technical one. When Sloane builds the analytics pipeline using Python libraries, she needs to check every dependency's licence before we deliver the codebase to a client.

Freeware and free software are not the same concept and confusing them is a common junior mistake. Freeware is software distributed at no cost, but the source code is not available and redistribution may be restricted. Free software (in the 'free as in freedom' sense) means the source code is available, modifiable, and redistributable — but this freedom comes with licence obligations you must honour. Always read the licence before you redistribute, modify, or embed someone else's code.

items.definition: A restrictive licence where the software owner retains all rights. Users may only use the software as explicitly permitted (e.g. on a set number of machines). Modifying, redistributing, or reverse-engineering is typically prohibited. Examples: Adobe CC, Microsoft Office.
A licence that grants users the right to view, modify, and distribute the source code, subject to conditions that vary by licence type. Examples: MIT, GPL, Apache 2.0.
A 'copyleft' open-source licence requiring that any software incorporating GPL code must also be distributed under the GPL with source code available. Creates a viral effect on derivative works.
A permissive open-source licence that allows use, modification, and redistribution in proprietary software with minimal conditions — typically just attribution in the licence notice.
Software distributed at no cost but with source code closed and redistribution often restricted. The developer retains copyright. Examples: Skype (historically), WinRAR (technically shareware).
Software distributed freely for a trial period or with limited features, requiring payment to unlock full functionality or continued use. A distribution model, not a rights framework.

rows: Yes — but derivative must also be GPL
Yes — but source must be released

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text: The GPL trap catches studios every year. A junior pulls in a GPL library for a client project — it is free, it works, it ships. Six months later the client wants to sell their product. Their legal team discovers that because the product includes GPL code, the entire codebase must be open-sourced under GPL. That is potentially millions of dollars of IP made public. Your job is to check licence compatibility before the code goes in, not after. Maintain a LICENCE.md file in every project listing every dependency and its licence type. I review this file in every PR. If it is missing, the PR is rejected.

items.label: Proprietary Software (e.g. Adobe CC, Microsoft Office)
points: Predictable, professional-grade support with SLAs
Regular updates included in subscription cost
Legal clarity — licence terms are explicit and enforceable
Cost: Adobe CC all-apps is ~$90 AUD/month per user
Lock-in risk: proprietary file formats (.ai, .psd) may not be portable
Cannot inspect or modify source code — must trust vendor's security

items.label: Open-Source Software (e.g. GIMP, LibreOffice, Audacity)
points: No licence cost — significant savings at studio scale
Full source code available — can audit security and customise behaviour
Community-driven development: features depend on volunteer priorities
Licence compliance obligations vary — must read and document each licence
Professional support requires paid third-party contracts or in-house expertise
Format compatibility with proprietary tools may require additional workflow steps

blocks.title: Software Licence Audit — Studio Compliance Report
blocks.software: VS Code / Google Sheets
blocks.intro: Complete a licence audit for the Bunbury Tech Launchpad studio. You will research licence types and produce a compliance report in spreadsheet format.
steps: Open Google Sheets and create a new spreadsheet titled 'Studio Licence Audit — Bunbury Tech Launchpad.'
Create the following column headers: Software Name | Version | Licence Type | Cost Model | Number of Seats/Installs | Compliant? (Y/N) | Notes / Action Required.

Add the following software to your audit (you will need to research each): GIMP 2.10, Audacity 3.4, Google Chrome, Adobe Photoshop CC, Microsoft Word (Office 365), Python 3.12, VS Code, Canva Pro, LibreOffice Calc, Inkscape.
For each item, research the licence type using the official software website. Identify whether it is proprietary, open-source (and which specific licence), freeware, or freemium.

In the 'Compliant?' column, mark as 'N' any software that is installed on more machines than the licence permits, or where the studio does not hold a valid licence. Mark known open-source tools as 'Y' with the licence name in Notes.
Add a summary row at the bottom: count the number of compliant tools, non-compliant tools, and total estimated annual cost for all paid licences at 1 seat.

In a second sheet tab, write a 150-word executive summary for the studio manager explaining the three highest-risk compliance issues and recommended actions. Present this as if you are Rory briefing the team.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · LICENCE SCENARIO DECISIONS
blocks.text:
Rory has put three real-world licence decisions on your desk. Each one requires a written recommendation with justification.

Scenario 1 — Sloane wants to use a Python library licenced under LGPL v2.1 in a proprietary commercial analytics tool we are building for a client. She says 'it is open source so it is fine.' Is she correct? What are the actual LGPL obligations and do they affect our proprietary codebase?

Scenario 2 — Leo found a font pack labelled 'Free for personal use' on a design resource website. He wants to use it in the festival campaign materials that we will be paid to produce. Is this permitted? What licence type should Leo specifically look for?

Scenario 3 — Sparky wants to fork a GitHub repository licenced under MIT, make modifications, and redistribute the modified version as part of a paid hardware toolkit. What does the MIT licence require him to do, and what can he NOT do?

Write a one-paragraph recommendation for each scenario.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS REVIEWING YOUR SPRINT
blocks.text:
Three questions before I sign off on the compliance report:

1. You listed GIMP as 'GPL v3' in the audit. If our studio modifies GIMP's source code to add a custom tool for our workflow, are we obligated to publish those modifications? Under what specific conditions does the GPL obligation to share source code apply?

2. The previous junior's font pack issue: a font is typically licenced separately from software under a font-specific licence or Creative Commons. Why does 'free for personal use' not cover our commercial client deliverables, and what is the legal risk if a client discovers we used a non-commercial font in their brand materials?

3. You recommended replacing Adobe Premiere (unlicensed) with DaVinci Resolve (free tier). What due diligence steps should you take before recommending an open-source or free tool as a professional replacement — beyond checking that it 'does the same thing'?

question.text: A developer incorporates a library licenced under GNU GPL v3 into a commercial software product. The product is then sold to customers. Which of the following statements is correct?
options: The developer must make the product free of charge because it includes GPL code
The developer must release the product's complete source code under GPL v3
The developer may keep the source code proprietary as long as they attribute the GPL library
The developer is not affected because they are only using the library, not modifying it
question.explanation: The GPL's 'copyleft' provision requires that any software incorporating GPL-licenced code must itself be distributed under the GPL — which means the source code must be made available. The developer can still charge for the software, but cannot keep the source proprietary. This is the 'viral' effect of the GPL that distinguishes it from permissive licences like MIT.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #3003 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟠 REVIEW | Sprint 14 | Assigned to: Junior Developer

Sparky's development machine crashed mid-sprint because a Windows update ran automatically and rebooted the machine, corrupting an unsaved Unity project. Leo cannot open his Canva designs from two months ago because his subscription lapsed and he exported nothing. Sloane's Python environment has three conflicting library versions that are breaking her data pipeline.

All three problems are software management failures — update mismanagement, no export/backup discipline, and uncontrolled dependency environments. Your sprint task: produce a studio software management policy document covering updates, backups, and environment management. Then implement the correct tools and procedures on your own machine. This is infrastructure, and infrastructure is your responsibility.
Software Management: Keeping the Studio Running

Software management covers the full lifecycle of software in a professional environment: acquisition, installation, configuration, updating, troubleshooting, and retirement. At a studio scale, poor software management creates compounding technical debt — outdated dependencies introduce security vulnerabilities, uncontrolled updates break working pipelines, and unmanaged storage fills disks mid-project. None of these are 'IT problems': they are delivery risks that sit on the project manager's radar.

Update management is one of the most misunderstood responsibilities in a junior role. Automatic updates are not always desirable in a professional environment. A major version update to a production tool mid-project can break file compatibility, change UI workflows, or remove features the team depends on. The professional approach is to maintain a tested stable version on production machines and evaluate updates in a separate test environment before rolling them out. Critical security patches are the exception — these should be applied promptly regardless of timing.

Virtual environments and dependency management tools (like Python's venv and pip, or Node's package.json) exist specifically to solve the 'it works on my machine' problem. Sloane's conflicting library versions happened because she installed everything into the global Python environment. The fix is simple but must be habitual: every project gets its own isolated environment, and that environment's dependencies are committed to the project repository as a requirements file. When another developer checks out the project, they recreate the exact same environment. Reproducibility is not optional at a professional studio.

items.definition: A system of numbering software releases to communicate the nature and scope of changes. Semantic versioning (SemVer) uses MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH format — e.g. 3.2.1. A major version change may include breaking changes.
An external library, framework, or tool that a software project relies on to function. Managing dependencies includes tracking versions, resolving conflicts, and ensuring licence compatibility.

An isolated Python (or other language) environment with its own interpreter and packages, independent of the global system installation. Created with `python -m venv env` in Python. Prevents dependency conflicts between projects.
The process of reverting to a previous version of software after an update causes problems. Requires version-controlled configuration or installation media for the prior version.
A best-practice backup policy: keep 3 copies of data, on 2 different media types, with 1 copy offsite or in the cloud. Protects against hardware failure, ransomware, and localised disasters.
A systematic review of all software installed on systems in an organisation, including version numbers, licence status, and usage. Used for compliance, security, and cost management.

rows: Auto-updates break a working pipeline mid-project
Disable auto-updates on production machines; stage updates in a test environment first; schedule update windows between sprints
Windows Update settings; macOS System Preferences; apt-hold (Linux)
Global pip installs create version conflicts across projects
Create a virtual environment (venv) per project; generate requirements.txt; commit to repo
Mismatched node_modules cause build failures across team members
Commit package-lock.json to repo; use npm ci (not npm install) in CI pipelines
Unsaved work lost to power failure or accidental deletion
Auto-save enabled; version control for code; cloud sync for design files; 3-2-1 backup rule
Git; Google Drive; OneDrive; Time Machine; Backblaze
Maintain a living software register; review quarterly
Google Sheets audit; Snipe-IT (open source asset manager)
Apply critical security patches promptly regardless of sprint phase; maintain a patch log

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · RORY QUINN, BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
