blocks.text: Sparky lost three days of Unity work to an auto-update reboot. Three days. That is half a sprint. The machine was not set up as a production environment — it was set up like a home PC. In a studio, every machine has a role: development machines have controlled update schedules, production rendering machines do not receive updates during active projects, and test machines receive all updates first. This is not overcomplicated — it is a one-time configuration that saves you weeks of pain across a year. Set it up once, document it in the studio wiki, and move on. The machine should be working for the project, not against it.

blocks.title: Python Virtual Environment — Standard Project Setup
blocks.defaultCode:
# Step 1: Create a virtual environment in the project folder
python -m venv env

# Step 2: Activate the environment
# Windows:
env\Scripts\activate
# macOS / Linux:
source env/bin/activate

# Step 3: Install project dependencies
pip install pandas matplotlib openpyxl

# Step 4: Freeze the dependency list to a file
pip freeze > requirements.txt

# The requirements.txt file now contains:
# pandas==2.1.4
# matplotlib==3.8.2
# openpyxl==3.1.2
# (plus all their sub-dependencies)

# Step 5: Another developer recreates the exact environment
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Step 6: Deactivate the environment when done
deactivate
blocks.challenge: Sloane's data pipeline uses pandas 1.5.3, but a new project requires pandas 2.1.4. Without virtual environments, installing 2.1.4 breaks the old pipeline. Modify this setup to create two separate environments — `env-legacy` (pandas 1.5.3) and `env-new` (pandas 2.1.4) — and write the command to switch between them on Windows. Then explain in a comment why `pip install -r requirements.txt` is safer than `pip install pandas` for a shared team project.

blocks.title: Studio Software Management Policy Implementation
blocks.software: Windows Settings / Google Sheets / VS Code terminal
blocks.intro: Implement the core software management practices on your own machine and document them for the studio policy.
steps: Open Windows Settings > Windows Update > Advanced Options. Disable 'Receive updates for other Microsoft products.' Set Active Hours to cover your typical work hours so reboots cannot occur during work time. Take a screenshot.
Open VS Code's integrated terminal. Navigate to your Documents folder: cd ~/Documents
Create a new project folder: mkdir rory-mgmt-demo and navigate into it: cd rory-mgmt-demo
Create a Python virtual environment: python -m venv env
Activate it (Windows): env\Scripts\activate — you should see (env) appear at the start of your prompt.
Install two libraries: pip install requests beautifulsoup4
Generate a requirements file: pip freeze > requirements.txt
Open requirements.txt in VS Code and inspect the contents. Note every library listed, including sub-dependencies you did not explicitly install.
Deactivate the environment: deactivate
Open Google Sheets and create a software management register for your own machine: list five applications, their versions, last update date, and whether auto-update is enabled or controlled.

In a new VS Code file called STUDIO-POLICY.md, write three short sections: (1) Update Management Policy, (2) Dependency Management Policy, (3) Backup Policy. Base each on the practices from this lesson. This becomes part of the studio wiki.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · TROUBLESHOOT SLOANE'S ENVIRONMENT
blocks.text:
Sloane's requirements.txt (from her global environment) is a disaster. Here is what it contains:

pandas==1.5.3
pandas==2.1.4
numpy==1.23.0
numpy==1.26.4
requests==2.28.1
openpyxl==3.0.10
flask==2.2.2
flask==3.0.0

Task 1: Identify every conflict in this file. Explain why pip will fail to install from this requirements.txt.

Task 2: Sloane says she needs pandas 2.1.4 for the new dashboard project, but her legacy ETL script requires pandas 1.5.3. Write the complete terminal commands (Windows) to create TWO separate virtual environments that resolve this conflict without either script breaking.

Task 3: Write a three-bullet 'requirements.txt hygiene' rule set for the studio wiki that would prevent this situation from recurring. Each rule should be actionable, not generic.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS REVIEWING YOUR SPRINT
blocks.text:
Three questions before the policy document goes to the studio manager:

1. You recommend disabling auto-updates on production machines. A security researcher just published a critical zero-day vulnerability in Windows that is being actively exploited. Does your policy cover this exception? Write the specific policy clause that handles emergency security patches differently from standard feature updates.

2. The 3-2-1 backup rule says one copy must be offsite or in the cloud. Leo stores his design files on his studio workstation and a USB drive he keeps in his desk drawer. How many of the 3-2-1 criteria does this meet, and what specific risk does the current setup leave Leo exposed to?

3. You committed a requirements.txt to the project repository. It includes the `env/` folder in the same commit. Why is committing the `env/` folder itself a problem, and how should it be handled instead?

question.text: Sloane runs `pip install pandas` on her studio machine and it updates pandas from version 1.5.3 to 2.1.4 globally. Her legacy ETL script immediately breaks with an ImportError. What is the correct long-term solution to prevent this class of problem?
options: Avoid updating any Python libraries to maintain stability
Use a separate Python virtual environment for each project, with a requirements.txt file specifying exact versions
Uninstall the new version and reinstall the old version each time she switches projects
Use two separate computers — one for legacy projects and one for new projects
question.explanation: Virtual environments isolate each project's dependencies from the global Python installation and from other projects. A requirements.txt file with pinned version numbers (e.g. pandas==1.5.3) ensures every developer recreates the exact same environment. This is the professional standard. Manually switching versions is error-prone and does not scale. Using separate computers is wasteful and still does not address the underlying configuration management problem.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #3004 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 IN PROGRESS | Sprint 15 | Assigned to: Junior Developer

Sloane has handed you the festival budget tracker and it is a mess. Hardcoded numbers everywhere, formulas that reference cells by address with no named ranges, no data validation, and a 'Total' column that adds a fixed number of rows — which means it silently breaks every time someone adds a new budget line.

The studio uses spreadsheets as mini-applications, not just tables. Clients interact with them directly, which means they must be robust, self-validating, and readable without the original author present. Your sprint task: rebuild the festival budget tracker as a professional-grade spreadsheet using named ranges, structured formulas, conditional formatting, and data validation. Sloane will stress-test it on Friday.
Spreadsheets as Applications: The Y12 Mindset Shift

At Year 12, the shift from 'using a spreadsheet' to 'designing a spreadsheet' requires treating the file as a product. A well-designed spreadsheet separates data entry from calculation from presentation, uses consistent naming conventions, validates inputs before they cause downstream errors, and works correctly even when the user does something unexpected. This is called defensive spreadsheet design, and it is standard practice in finance, operations, and project management.

Named ranges transform a spreadsheet from a grid of cell references into something readable. A formula like `=SUMIF(Category,"Design",Amount)` is immediately understandable; `=SUMIF(B2:B47,"Design",D2:D47)` requires the reader to navigate to those cells to understand what they contain. Named ranges also make formulas resilient to structural changes — if rows are inserted or deleted, named ranges adjust automatically. This is not a comfort feature: it is a correctness feature.

Nested IF functions and lookup formulas (VLOOKUP, XLOOKUP, INDEX/MATCH) are the logic layer of spreadsheet design. They allow a spreadsheet to respond intelligently to input — classifying budget items, flagging overruns, retrieving data from reference tables without manual copying. At Y12 level, you are expected to construct these formulas from first principles, debug them when they return unexpected results, and explain the logic to a non-technical stakeholder.

items.definition: A label assigned to a cell or range of cells (e.g. naming B2:B100 as 'Category'). Named ranges make formulas readable, reduce reference errors, and automatically adjust when rows are inserted or deleted.

A function that searches the first column of a table for a value and returns data from a specified column in the same row. Syntax: =VLOOKUP(lookup_value, table_array, col_index_num, [range_lookup]). Superseded by XLOOKUP in newer versions of Excel/Sheets.
SUMIF sums values in a range where a corresponding range meets a single condition. SUMIFS supports multiple conditions. Syntax: =SUMIF(range, criteria, sum_range).

A spreadsheet feature that automatically changes the appearance (colour, font, border) of cells based on their value or formula result. Used to visually highlight overruns, anomalies, or status indicators.

A spreadsheet feature that restricts what values can be entered into a cell — for example, a dropdown list of approved categories, or a rule that only allows numbers between 0 and 100,000. Prevents input errors at the source.

A relative reference (e.g. A1) adjusts when a formula is copied to another cell. An absolute reference (e.g. $A$1) does not adjust. Mixed references ($A1 or A$1) lock one dimension only. Critical to understand for formulas that are dragged across ranges.

blocks.title: Advanced Spreadsheet Formulas — Festival Budget Tracker
blocks.challenge: The studio manager wants to see the percentage of the total budget consumed by each category. Using the named ranges above, write a formula for a 'Budget Share %' column that calculates each category's Budgeted amount as a percentage of the grand total. Use an absolute reference for the total so the formula can be dragged down without breaking. Then write a SUMIFS formula that totals Actual spend ONLY for items where Category is 'Design' AND Status is 'Over Budget'.

rows: Sums values where one condition is met
Sums where multiple conditions are all met
Forgetting that sum_range comes FIRST in SUMIFS (opposite to COUNTIFS)
Returns value from a table at a specified column
Using TRUE (approximate match) when exact match is needed; table not sorted
Modern replacement for VLOOKUP; searches any column
Not available in Excel 2016 or earlier
Returns different values based on multiple conditions
Exceeding 7 nested levels; forgetting closing parentheses
Cell reference does not shift when formula is copied
Forgetting $ signs when a formula is dragged across rows/columns

blocks.title: Rebuild the Festival Budget Tracker
blocks.software: Microsoft Excel / Google Sheets
blocks.intro: You will build a professional-grade budget tracker from scratch using the design principles and formulas covered in this lesson.
steps: Open a new Google Sheets (or Excel) file. Name it 'Festival Budget Tracker v2 — Bunbury Tech Launchpad'.
Create a 'Data' sheet tab. Add these column headers in row 1: Item ID | Category | Description | Budgeted ($) | Actual ($) | Variance ($) | Status | Supplier Code.
Add at least 10 rows of sample festival budget data across four categories: Design, Logistics, Technology, Marketing.

Select column B (Category). Go to Data > Data Validation. Set the validation type to 'List of items' and enter: Design,Logistics,Technology,Marketing. This creates a dropdown and prevents invalid input.
In column F (Variance), enter the formula =D2-E2 for the first data row. Do NOT hardcode numbers. Copy this formula down all data rows.
In column G (Status), enter the nested IF formula from the codePreview block. Adjust the thresholds if needed for your data. Copy down.
Select column G. Apply conditional formatting: red fill for 'Over Budget,' orange fill for 'At Risk,' green fill for 'On Track.'
Select columns B through H (the data range). Go to Data > Named Ranges (Sheets) or Formulas > Name Manager (Excel). Define the named ranges: Category, Description, Budgeted, Actual, Variance, Status.
Create a second sheet tab called 'Summary.' Add a category breakdown table with SUMIF formulas for Budgeted and Actual amounts for each of the four categories.
Add a 'Grand Total' row at the bottom of the summary using SUM formulas. Add a 'Budget Used %' row: =SUM(Actual)/SUM(Budgeted) formatted as a percentage.
Test the data validation: attempt to type an invalid category in column B. Confirm the dropdown appears and rejects invalid text.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · SPREADSHEET STRESS TEST
blocks.text:
Sloane is stress-testing your budget tracker. Here are the four things she is going to try:

Test 1 — She inserts a new row in the middle of the data range. Do your SUMIF formulas still capture the new row? Do your named ranges expand to include it? (Hint: named ranges in Google Sheets do NOT automatically expand when rows are inserted inside them.)

Test 2 — She types 'design' (lowercase) in the Category column. Does your SUMIF pick it up, or is it case-sensitive? (Google Sheets SUMIF is not case-sensitive; Excel is. How does this affect your formula design?)

Test 3 — She enters a negative number in the Budgeted column. Your Status formula uses 'Over Budget' when Variance < -500. What does a negative budget create as output, and how would you add input validation to prevent it?

Test 4 — She wants to add a 'Q1 Budget' and 'Q2 Budget' split. You currently have a single Budgeted column. What structural change to the spreadsheet would support quarterly tracking without breaking existing formulas?

Answer each test as a written troubleshooting note.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS REVIEWING YOUR SPRINT
blocks.text:
Three questions before Sloane gets this tracker:

1. Your VLOOKUP formula references Sheet2!$A:$C — the entire columns A through C. On a spreadsheet with 10,000 rows of supplier data, how does searching an entire column range affect spreadsheet performance compared to referencing a defined table range like Sheet2!$A$1:$C$500? What feature in Excel Tables (Ctrl+T) would make this reference both readable and dynamically sized?

2. You used a nested IF with three levels to produce 'On Track / At Risk / Over Budget.' The studio manager now wants five status levels. At what point does a nested IF become unmanageable, and what alternative formula approach (using a lookup table) would produce the same result while being easier to maintain?

3. The original tracker had hardcoded totals that silently broke when rows were added. You fixed this with named ranges and SUMIF. But a junior team member could still break it by accidentally deleting the named range definition. What two protective measures can you apply to the spreadsheet to prevent accidental modification of the formula infrastructure?

question.text: A spreadsheet formula in cell G2 is: =SUMIF(Category, "Design", Budgeted). When this formula is copied to cell G3, what happens?
options: The formula breaks because named ranges cannot be used in SUMIF
The formula in G3 becomes =SUMIF(Category, "Design", Budgeted) — identical, because named ranges do not shift when copied
The formula adjusts to =SUMIF(Category, "Marketing", Budgeted) because the criteria argument shifts down one row
The formula returns an error because the criteria must be a cell reference, not a text string
question.explanation: Named ranges behave like absolute references — they refer to the same cells regardless of where the formula is copied. When you copy =SUMIF(Category, "Design", Budgeted) to any cell, it continues to reference the same named ranges and produces the same result. This is one of the key advantages of named ranges over cell address references, where SUMIF(B2:B100, "Design", D2:D100) would shift incorrectly when copied.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #3005 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🔴 URGENT | Sprint 16 | Assigned to: Junior Developer

Sloane has pulled 1,847 rows of festival ticket sales data from the event platform. The client wants answers by 9 AM tomorrow: which ticket category generated the highest revenue, which day of the week had peak sales, and which postcode cluster is underperforming. The data is raw — inconsistent capitalisation in the Category column, blank rows, duplicate transaction IDs, and dates stored as text strings.

Your task: clean the dataset, build pivot tables answering the three questions, and produce a one-page executive summary with supporting charts. Sloane says if you sort without filtering duplicates first, your pivot will lie to you. She is correct. Work systematically — data cleaning before analysis, always.
Data Analysis Workflow: Clean First, Analyse Second

Data analysis in a professional context follows a strict sequence: data cleaning, then transformation, then analysis, then visualisation. Skipping or reordering steps is the fastest way to produce confidently wrong answers. A pivot table built on dirty data — duplicates, inconsistent categories, blank rows — produces numbers that look authoritative but are misleading. The client makes a decision based on those numbers, and when the error is discovered, the trust you spent months building is gone in one meeting.

Sorting and filtering are not just navigational tools — they are analytical techniques. Multi-level sorting (sort by Category, then by Date, then by Revenue) reveals patterns invisible in unsorted data. Advanced filter criteria with AND/OR logic allow you to isolate specific subsets for comparison. At Y12 level, you are expected to use these tools intentionally, not randomly, and to document your analytical decisions so another team member can reproduce your results.

Pivot tables are the most powerful analytical tool available in a spreadsheet application, and most users use approximately 20% of their capability. A pivot table does not just summarise data — it allows you to dynamically reframe the same dataset from multiple analytical angles without writing a single formula. The key skill is choosing the right field for each pivot zone (Rows, Columns, Values, Filters) based on the analytical question you are answering, not based on how the data happens to be organised.

items.definition: An interactive data summarisation tool that aggregates, groups, and cross-tabulates large datasets without modifying the source data. Allows dynamic reorganisation of analysis by dragging fields between Rows, Columns, Values, and Filters.

The process of identifying and correcting errors, inconsistencies, and missing values in a dataset before analysis. Includes removing duplicates, standardising formats, handling blanks, and correcting data types.
TRIM removes leading, trailing, and extra internal spaces from text. CLEAN removes non-printable characters. Both are essential for standardising imported data before analysis or matching.
A sort operation that orders data by multiple columns in sequence. For example: sort by Category A→Z, then within each category sort by Revenue largest→smallest.

A filtering mode (available in Excel) that allows complex filter criteria — including AND/OR combinations and wildcard matching — defined in a separate criteria range rather than through dropdown menus.
Summary measures that describe a dataset's central tendency and spread: mean (average), median (middle value), mode (most frequent value), minimum, maximum, and standard deviation.

rows: Sort by transaction ID; visually scan for repeats
Select data range > Data > Remove Duplicates (Excel) or Data > Data Cleanup > Remove Duplicates (Sheets)
Sort the Category column and scan visually
Add a helper column with =PROPER(B2) or =UPPER(B2); paste-special as values to replace originals
Dates left-align instead of right-align; sort does not work chronologically
Use =DATEVALUE(A2) to convert; or Text to Columns wizard in Excel
VLOOKUP returns no match despite apparent identical values
Apply =TRIM(A2) in a helper column; paste-special as values
Pivot table includes a blank category; counts are off
Filter for blanks (filter > sort > blank check); delete blank rows; or use Go To Special > Blanks
Select range > Data > Text to Columns > Finish (forces conversion); or multiply by 1

blocks.title: Data Analysis Formulas — Festival Ticket Sales
blocks.challenge: Sloane needs a formula that calculates the average revenue per transaction for each Category, without using a pivot table — purely using worksheet formulas. Write an AVERAGEIF formula to do this. Then write a second formula that identifies the category with the HIGHEST average transaction revenue, using a combination of AVERAGEIF and MATCH (or INDEX/MATCH). Explain in a comment why you would still prefer a pivot table for this task in a production context.

blocks.title: Festival Ticket Sales Analysis
blocks.software: Microsoft Excel / Google Sheets
blocks.intro: Work through the complete data analysis pipeline on the provided ticket sales dataset. Follow the clean-transform-analyse-visualise sequence.
steps: Open the provided festival-tickets-raw.xlsx file (or create a sample dataset with 20+ rows: columns TransactionID, Date, Category, Quantity, UnitPrice, Revenue, Postcode).
Data cleaning step: Use COUNTIF to flag duplicate TransactionIDs in a helper column. Formula: =COUNTIF($A$2:$A$2000,A2)>1. Filter for TRUE values and delete duplicate rows.
Standardise the Category column: add a helper column with =PROPER(TRIM(B2)). Copy the helper column, paste-special as Values only over the original Category column, then delete the helper column.
Check the Date column: if dates are left-aligned, they are stored as text. Add a helper column with =DATEVALUE(C2), format as Date, paste-special as Values to replace the text dates.
Add a DayOfWeek column: =TEXT(C2, "dddd") to extract the day name from each date.
Insert a Pivot Table (Insert > PivotTable). Place it on a new sheet called 'Analysis.'
Pivot Table 1 — Revenue by Category: drag Category to Rows, Revenue to Values (Sum). Sort by Sum of Revenue descending.
Pivot Table 2 — Revenue by Day of Week: drag DayOfWeek to Rows, Revenue to Values (Sum), Quantity to Values (Sum). Identify peak day.
Pivot Table 3 — Revenue by Postcode: drag Postcode to Rows, Revenue to Values (Sum). Sort descending. Identify the bottom 5 postcodes.
Create a bar chart from Pivot Table 1 and a line chart from Pivot Table 2. Label axes, add a title, and remove chart junk (unnecessary gridlines, legend if redundant).
On a new sheet called 'Executive Summary,' write three bullet points answering the client's three questions, citing the specific numbers from your pivot tables.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · SLOANE'S DATA QUALITY CHALLENGE
blocks.text:
Sloane has sent you a second dataset: postcode-performance.csv. It has three problems she wants you to diagnose and fix before the client presentation.

Problem 1: The Revenue column contains values formatted as '$1,234.56' (text with dollar sign and comma). When you SUM the column it returns 0. Write the Excel formula that extracts the numeric value from this text string. (Hint: you will need SUBSTITUTE and VALUE.)

Problem 2: The Postcode column contains a mix of 4-digit postcodes (correct: '6230') and 3-digit postcodes (likely missing a leading zero: '623' instead of '0623'). Write a formula that detects postcodes shorter than 4 characters and displays the corrected version with a leading zero. (Hint: LEN and TEXT functions.)

Problem 3: The Category column has three spellings of the same thing: 'Gen Admission', 'General Admission', and 'GA'. Sloane wants all three standardised to 'General Admission'. Write a formula using nested SUBSTITUTE or IFS that normalises all three spellings. Show the formula logic step by step.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS REVIEWING YOUR SPRINT
blocks.text:
Three questions before the executive summary goes to the client:

1. Your pivot table shows Saturday as the highest revenue day. But Sloane points out that there were three Saturdays in the dataset and only one Friday. Is total revenue the right metric to compare day-of-week performance, or should you be using average revenue per occurrence? What change to the pivot table's Value Field Settings would give a fairer comparison?

2. You reported 'Postcode 6230 is underperforming.' The dataset has 847 transactions in postcode 6230 and only 12 in postcode 6232. Is 'underperforming' a meaningful comparison when the transaction counts are so different? What additional metric would make this comparison valid?

3. The client wants to filter the pivot table to show only transactions above $100 revenue. You applied a manual filter on the source data. Why is filtering the source data the wrong approach here, and what pivot-table-specific tool should you use instead?

question.text: Sloane runs =SUM(D2:D1848) on a Revenue column and gets 0, even though the column appears to contain numbers. What is the most likely cause?
options: The SUM formula has the wrong range — it should reference the entire column
The numbers in column D are stored as text (e.g. imported with a dollar sign or comma), so SUM treats them as non-numeric
Google Sheets and Excel use different SUM syntax, causing the formula to fail
The column has conditional formatting applied, which interferes with the SUM calculation
question.explanation: When numbers are stored as text — for example, imported as '$1,234.56' or '1,234' with a comma — Excel and Sheets cannot perform arithmetic on them. SUM ignores text values and returns 0. The fix is to use =VALUE(SUBSTITUTE(SUBSTITUTE(D2,"$",""),",","")) to strip the formatting characters and convert to a true number, or use the Text to Columns tool to force conversion. Conditional formatting has no effect on calculated values.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #3006 · BUNBURY TECH LAUNCHPAD
blocks.text:
Status: 🔴 SPRINT FINAL | Sprint 17 | Assigned to: Junior Developer

This is the capstone sprint. The Collie River Festival campaign ships on Friday. Every team member's output must be integrated into a single cohesive deliverable: Leo's SVG assets, Sparky's hardware display config, Sloane's analytics dashboard data, and your own spreadsheet models and project documentation.

Integrated digital production means the whole is greater than the sum of the parts — but only if the parts are compatible. You need to manage the handoffs, resolve the format conflicts, ensure version control is active across all assets, and produce a final delivery package that a client can actually open, use, and maintain after we hand it over. Rory's rule: the client does not care about your internal process. They care that everything works on the day.
Integrated Production: Managing Digital Handoffs

Integrated digital production is the discipline of combining outputs from multiple tools, team members, and media types into a unified deliverable. In a professional studio, this means managing asset handoffs (Leo's designs into the web build), format compatibility (Sparky's hardware requirements vs. web delivery requirements), version control (ensuring the team is working from the same source files), and quality assurance (testing the integrated output against the client's actual requirements, not your assumptions about them).

The most common failure point in integrated production is the assumption of compatibility. Leo designed the hero banner at 1920×1080 px, but the CMS requires 1200×630 px for social sharing meta tags. Sparky's LED controller accepts SVG 1.1, but Leo exported SVG 1.2 with features the controller does not support. These are not individual errors — they are coordination failures that a project manager must anticipate, not react to. The fix is a technical specification document agreed before any production begins, not a rush conversion the night before delivery.

Version control is not optional in an integrated production environment. When Sloane updates the data model, Leo's chart assets may need to be re-exported. When Sparky changes the hardware display dimensions, Leo's SVG layout must be updated. Without version-controlled assets and a clear asset dependency map, these cascading changes create chaos. At minimum, every project should use a shared folder with clearly named version suffixes (_v1, _v2, _FINAL, _FINAL-v2 — the last one being a red flag that the versioning discipline has already broken down).

items.definition: The defined sequence of tools and processes through which a raw asset (e.g. a photograph or audio recording) passes to reach its final delivered form. A well-defined pipeline includes format specifications at each stage.

A document agreed before production begins that defines file formats, dimensions, colour profiles, naming conventions, and delivery requirements for all assets. Prevents compatibility failures at handoff.

A system for tracking changes to files over time, allowing the team to identify who changed what, when, and why — and to revert to previous versions if needed. Git is the professional standard for code; shared drives with naming conventions serve this role for design assets.

The transfer of an asset or deliverable from one team member or tool to another. A clean handoff includes the asset in the correct format, at the correct specification, with any necessary documentation about how it was created.

The systematic process of testing a deliverable against its requirements before client delivery. For integrated digital production, QA includes cross-device testing, format validation, link checking, and review against the original brief.

The final set of files delivered to a client, organised so the client can understand, use, and maintain the deliverable independently. Typically includes assets, documentation, source files, and a README explaining the folder structure.

rows: Sparky's LED controller rejects SVG 1.2 features used by Leo
Agree SVG version and feature set in tech spec before production
Re-export SVG from Leo's source file with compatibility settings; test in controller simulator
Hero banner is 1920×1080 but CMS social share requires 1200×630
List all required dimensions per platform in tech spec
Batch export multiple sizes from Leo's master file; do not resize rasterised exports
Leo designs in P3 wide gamut; website renders in sRGB — colours shift noticeably
Specify sRGB as the production colour space in the tech spec
Convert Leo's files to sRGB before export; check in browser
Enforce naming convention at project kickoff: component-variant-version.ext
Audit delivery folder; rename all assets according to convention before packaging
Sloane updates the sales CSV; Leo's chart images are now out of date
Identify asset dependencies at kickoff; flag data-driven assets for update when source data changes
Re-export all data-driven assets from the updated source; version the new exports
Client cannot identify which file is the editable source and which is the export
Include a README in the delivery package explaining every file's purpose and tool required to edit it
Create retrospectively from team knowledge; add to delivery package before handover

blocks.title: Tools for Integrated Digital Production at the Studio

items.label: Version Control: Git + GitHub
points: Industry-standard version control for code, configuration files, and text assets
Every change is logged with author, timestamp, and commit message
Branching allows parallel development without overwriting each other's work
Pull requests enable peer review before changes are merged to main
Not ideal for large binary files (PSD, MP4) — use Git LFS or alternative for media
Rory's requirement: every project repository includes a .gitignore, README, and LICENCE file

items.label: Design Handoff: Canva / Figma
points: Canva: Leo's primary tool — assets can be shared via link with edit or view permissions
Figma: industry standard for UI design handoff — developers can inspect design tokens without needing the app
Both generate shareable links instead of file attachments — single source of truth
Version history built into both tools — earlier versions are recoverable
Export formats configurable per asset: PNG, SVG, PDF, WebP
Limitation: if the link is broken or account access is revoked, the client loses access — always export a backup set

items.label: Asset Organisation: Shared Drive Structure
points: Google Drive / OneDrive: accessible to all team members and the client
Folder structure should mirror the delivery package: /source /exports /delivery
Naming convention enforced at project level: component-variant-vX.ext
Permissions: client gets read-only access to /delivery; team has edit on /source
Not a replacement for Git on code files — use both: Git for code, Drive for media
Backup rule: Drive is synced locally on all team machines — not a single-point-of-failure cloud-only store

blocks.title: Capstone: Build and Package the Festival Campaign Delivery
blocks.software: VS Code / Google Drive / Canva / Google Sheets / Audacity
blocks.intro: You will assemble all sprint outputs into a professional delivery package and perform quality assurance checks before the client handover.
steps: Create a project delivery folder structure in Google Drive: /collie-river-festival /source /exports /delivery /documentation
Move all source files (Leo's Canva designs, Sparky's hardware config, Sloane's CSV data, your spreadsheet models) into /source with correct version naming: component-variant-v1.ext
Produce final exports: export Leo's logo as SVG (for hardware), PNG at 600 dpi (for print), and WebP at 80% quality (for web). Place all exports in /exports.
Perform format QA: open each export in its target tool or browser. Confirm SVG opens correctly in Chrome (drag file into browser tab), PNG dimensions match spec, WebP renders in Chrome and Firefox.

Perform data QA: open Sloane's CSV in Google Sheets. Confirm no blank rows, no duplicate transaction IDs, correct postcode format. Run =COUNTA(A:A)-COUNTBLANK(A:A) to verify row count against the expected total.

Perform audio QA: open the exported voiceover-web.mp3 in Audacity. Confirm sample rate is 44,100 Hz, duration matches the script, and there are no clipping artefacts (peaks above 0 dBFS shown in red).
Create a README.md in VS Code for the delivery package. Include: project name, delivery date, client contact, file inventory (filename, format, purpose, tool needed to edit), and known limitations.
Move all QA-passed assets to /delivery. Confirm the folder contains ONLY delivery-ready files — no source PSDs, no draft versions, no _OLD files.
Share the /delivery folder with the client (Google Drive share link, view only). Test the link in an incognito browser window to confirm client-accessible.

Update the sprint board: mark Ticket #3001 through #3005 as Done, mark #3006 as Ready for Review. Write a one-paragraph sprint retrospective: what integration challenge took the most time, and what technical specification clause would have prevented it.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · PRE-DELIVERY QA CHECKLIST
blocks.text:
Rory has given you the studio's pre-delivery QA checklist. Work through each item for the festival campaign and record Pass / Fail / Not Applicable with a one-line note.

1. All assets in /delivery are in the correct format for their intended use (web, print, hardware). ✓/✗
2. No source or draft files are present in /delivery. ✓/✗
3. File naming follows the convention: component-variant-vX.ext for all assets. ✓/✗
4. README.md is present and lists every file with its purpose and the tool required to edit it. ✓/✗
5. The SVG logo opens and renders correctly in Chrome 120+ without JavaScript errors. ✓/✗
6. The MP3 voiceover plays in a browser audio element (test with a simple HTML file: <audio src='voiceover-web.mp3' controls>). ✓/✗
7. All spreadsheet formulas return expected results with the final production data (not test data). ✓/✗
8. The client's share link is view-only and opens correctly in an incognito browser window. ✓/✗
9. The sprint board has been updated with all ticket statuses and a retrospective note. ✓/✗
10. The studio licence register has been updated to include any new tools used in this sprint. ✓/✗

For any item that fails: write the specific remediation action and the team member responsible.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · RORY IS REVIEWING YOUR SPRINT
blocks.text:
Three questions before this project ships:

1. The README you wrote describes every file in /delivery, but a new team member asks: 'How do I regenerate the festival-revenue-chart.png if the underlying data changes?' The current README does not answer this question. What specific information about the asset's production process should a professional README include for any data-driven asset?

2. You shared the /delivery folder with the client using a Google Drive link set to 'Anyone with the link can view.' The client's marketing manager accidentally shared that link in a public Facebook post. What are the data exposure risks — given the delivery package includes postcode-level customer revenue data — and what permission model should be used for client deliverables containing personal or commercially sensitive data?

3. During sprint retrospective, you identify that the format conflict between Leo's SVG 1.2 export and Sparky's SVG 1.1 controller took four hours to resolve. Write a specific technical specification clause — two or three sentences in plain language — that you would add to the studio's project kickoff template to prevent this exact problem recurring on future projects.

question.text: At the end of a project, the /delivery folder contains the following files: hero-banner-FINAL.png, hero-banner-FINAL-v2.png, hero-banner-FINAL-v2-USE THIS ONE.png, logo.svg, logo_old.svg, voiceover.mp3, voiceover-web.mp3. Which of the following best describes the primary problem with this delivery package?
options: The folder contains both PNG and SVG formats, which is unnecessary duplication
The file naming convention has broken down, making it impossible to identify with confidence which version of each asset is the correct final deliverable
The MP3 file should be in WAV format for client delivery
The delivery package should only contain one file per asset type
question.explanation: The presence of 'FINAL,' 'FINAL-v2,' and 'FINAL-v2-USE THIS ONE' naming patterns indicates that file versioning discipline broke down during the project. A client (or future team member) cannot reliably identify which file to use. Professional practice requires a clear naming convention enforced throughout the project, and a /delivery folder that contains only the confirmed final versions with no ambiguity. Having multiple formats (PNG and SVG) can be entirely appropriate when each serves a different delivery purpose.

blocks.text: Understanding the Client Brief

In professional IT work, every project begins with a client brief — a document or conversation that defines what the client wants, why they want it, and what success looks like. Your ability to read and interpret a brief accurately determines whether the final product actually solves the client's problem.

blocks.heading: Career Path: Project Coordinator
blocks.text: Project coordinators spend a significant portion of their time clarifying requirements with clients. A misunderstood brief at the start of a project costs far more to fix at the end. In industry, this is called 'the cost of change' — the later a mistake is caught, the more expensive it is to fix.
What Is a Client Brief?

A client brief outlines the context and requirements for a project. It is the starting point for all planning decisions. In Applied IT, your brief may be provided by a teacher, a simulated client scenario, or — in industry placement — a real business client.

items.definition: A document or statement from the client describing the project purpose, requirements, constraints, and expected outcomes.
The boundaries of a project — what IS included and, importantly, what is NOT included.
The process of examining a brief to extract, clarify, and prioritise what the solution must do.
A limitation placed on the project — such as time, budget, software tools, or audience.

blocks.text: The Four Dimensions of a Brief
When you receive a brief, examine it through four lenses:

rows: What must be included? What is out of scope? How big is this project?
Prevents scope creep — the gradual expansion of project requirements beyond what was agreed.
When is it due? Are there milestones? How many work sessions do I have?
Drives your Gantt chart and task prioritisation.
What software, hardware, assets, or data do I have access to? What skills do I need?
Identifies gaps early — before they become problems.
Why does the client need this? Who will use it? What problem does it solve?
Ensures the solution is fit for purpose, not just technically complete.

blocks.text: Asking the Right Questions

Clients rarely provide every detail in a brief. Part of your job is to identify the gaps and ask targeted questions. Good questions are specific, open-ended, and focused on understanding — not on seeking approval.

items: Who specifically will be using the final product?
Are there existing brand guidelines, colours, or styles I need to follow?
What format should the final deliverable be in (print, web, video, app)?
Are there any content or features that are definitely NOT required?
What does a successful outcome look like to you?

blocks.heading: Project Manager Tip
blocks.text: Document every clarification. If a client answers a question verbally, follow up with a written summary: 'Just to confirm, you said the app does not need to work offline — is that correct?' This protects you if requirements change later.

blocks.title: Client Brief: Local Music Festival
blocks.scenario: You have been approached by the coordinator of a local community music festival. She says: 'We need something digital to promote the festival. We want people to know when it is, who is playing, and how to get tickets. Our audience is mainly 18–35 year olds and we use a lot of Instagram. Oh, and we need it done in three weeks — our graphic designer is busy so it's just you.' No other written documentation has been provided.

questions.question: Identify THREE pieces of information that are missing or unclear in this brief that you would need to clarify before starting work.
Write two targeted clarifying questions you would ask the client, explaining why each question is important for your planning.
Define the scope of this project in your own words, including at least one thing that is explicitly out of scope based on what the client has said.

blocks.text: Interpreting the Brief: Your Written Response

Once you have read and clarified the brief, you write an 'interpreted brief' — a restatement of the requirements in your own words. This shows the client (and your assessor) that you have understood the project, not just copied the brief.

items: State the purpose of the product (what it is and what problem it solves).
Describe the target audience (who will use it and what their needs are).
List the functional requirements (what it must do).
List the constraints (time, tools, format, content boundaries).
State your evaluation criteria (how you will know the solution is successful).

question.text: A client tells you verbally that the project does NOT need to include a mobile version. What is the most professionally appropriate next step?
options: Proceed with the project and leave mobile out, as instructed.
Send the client a written summary confirming that mobile is out of scope.
Include mobile anyway in case the client changes their mind.
Ask the client to rewrite the brief to include this detail.
question.explanation: Verbal agreements should always be confirmed in writing. Sending a summary protects both you and the client if requirements are disputed later — a standard practice in professional project management.

question.text: Explain the difference between scope and constraints in the context of a client brief. Give one example of each.

blocks.text: Planning Your Project

Professional project planning transforms a client brief into an actionable schedule. It forces you to break large, vague tasks into specific, time-bound actions — and it gives you an early warning when something is falling behind.

blocks.heading: Industry Standard
blocks.text: The Project Management Institute (PMI) reports that organisations with mature project planning practices waste 28 times less money on failed projects than those without. Planning is not busywork — it is the highest-leverage activity in any project.
Scope Creep: The Silent Project Killer

Scope creep occurs when the project gradually expands beyond what was originally agreed — one small addition at a time. Each individual addition seems reasonable, but together they blow out time and resources. Recognising and managing scope creep is one of the most important skills a project manager can have.

blocks.definition: The gradual, uncontrolled expansion of a project's scope beyond what was originally agreed, typically without corresponding adjustments to time or resources.

items: Signs of scope creep: 'Can we just add one more feature?', new requirements appearing after work has started, the brief being reinterpreted mid-project.
Prevention: Document the scope clearly at the start; get client sign-off on the interpreted brief.
Response: When new requirements arise, assess their impact on time and resources, then negotiate with the client — add time, remove another feature, or defer the addition to a future phase.

blocks.text: Time Management: Breaking Down the Work

Before you create a schedule, list every task required to complete the project. This is called a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS). A WBS turns 'create a website' into specific, estimable tasks like 'design homepage wireframe', 'source and edit hero image', 'write navigation labels'.

items.definition: A hierarchical decomposition of all the work required to complete a project, broken into manageable tasks.
A horizontal bar chart that plots project tasks on a timeline, showing start and end dates, duration, and task dependencies.
A significant checkpoint in a project — often a deliverable or decision point — that marks progress.
A relationship where one task cannot start (or finish) until another task is complete.

blocks.text: Reading a Gantt Chart

A Gantt chart has tasks listed vertically on the left and a calendar timeline across the top. Each task is shown as a horizontal bar spanning its start and end date. Milestones are often shown as diamonds. Dependency arrows show which tasks must finish before others can begin.

rows: Milestone — a key deliverable or checkpoint
Task dependency — B cannot start until A finishes
Build site cannot start until wireframes are approved
Task is delayed or at risk
Site build planned for Day 5 but started on Day 7

blocks.text: Resource Allocation

Resources are everything required to complete the project: time, people, software, hardware, stock images, fonts, data, and even physical space. Identifying resources early prevents being blocked mid-project because you lack access to a tool or asset.

rows: Adobe Illustrator, Figma, Microsoft Publisher, DaVinci Resolve
Confirm you have access and a valid licence before starting
Identify sources; check licence (royalty-free vs commercial)
School computers, drawing tablet, scanner, camera
Book equipment in advance if shared
Map to your Gantt chart; identify your critical path
Identify gaps early; plan learning time or seek support

blocks.heading: Project Manager Tip
blocks.text: When estimating how long tasks will take, use the '3-point estimation' technique: estimate the best case, the worst case, and the most likely time. Use the most likely estimate in your Gantt chart, but build buffer time before critical milestones.

blocks.title: Build Your Project Gantt Chart
blocks.software: Microsoft Excel / Google Sheets / Canva / GanttProject (free)
blocks.intro: Using a brief provided by your teacher (or the music festival scenario from Lesson 1), create a Gantt chart for the full project. Your chart must show at least 10 tasks, 2 milestones, and at least one dependency.
steps: List every task needed to complete the project. Aim for tasks that are 1–3 days in duration — if a task takes longer, break it down further.
Identify dependencies: which tasks cannot start until another is finished? Draw these relationships on paper first.
Estimate the time for each task using 3-point estimation (best/worst/likely). Record your estimates.
Identify your milestones: client sign-off on design, completion of first draft, final submission.
Plot all tasks and milestones on your Gantt chart, colour-coding by phase (plan / create / review / evaluate).
Add a 'resources' column to your task list, noting what you need for each task.
Share your chart with a classmate and ask them to identify any gaps, missing tasks, or unrealistic time estimates.
Annotate your chart with a brief note explaining two planning decisions you made (e.g., why you allowed 2 days for the wireframe review).

question.text: Halfway through a project, your client asks you to add three new pages to the website that were not in the original brief. What is the correct project management response?
options: Add the pages immediately — the client is always right.
Refuse to add anything that was not in the original brief.
Assess the impact on time and resources, then negotiate a scope change or timeline extension with the client.
Add the pages and work extra hours to keep the same deadline.
question.explanation: New requirements mid-project are a scope change. The professional response is to assess the impact, document it, and negotiate with the client — adjusting the deadline, removing other features, or agreeing to defer the additions to a future version.

question.text: A student has one week to create a promotional video. They spend the first three days filming without creating a Gantt chart. On day four they realise they have no time left for editing. Explain what project management technique would have prevented this problem, and describe how it works.

blocks.text: Design Representation Techniques

Before you build anything, you design it. Design representation techniques are the visual tools that let you plan, communicate, and test your design ideas before committing hours of build time. They are used by professional UX designers, web developers, video producers, and software engineers alike.

blocks.heading: Career Path: UX Designer
blocks.text: UX (User Experience) Designers spend most of their time in the design representation phase — sketching ideas, building wireframes, creating prototypes, and testing with users — before any final product is built. Mastering these techniques is a direct pathway to a career in UX, UI design, or digital product management.
Why Represent Your Design First?

items: Identify problems in the layout or user flow before they are built in — changes are free on paper, expensive in code.
Communicate your vision to clients and team members who may not read technical documentation.
Get client approval on the look and feel before investing build time.
Test usability: can a user find what they need without instruction?

blocks.text: Technique 1: Thumbnails

Thumbnails are rapid, small-scale sketches that explore multiple layout ideas quickly. Done by hand or digitally, they are intentionally rough — the goal is quantity of ideas, not quality of drawing. A designer might produce 10–20 thumbnails in 20 minutes before selecting 2–3 to develop further.

blocks.definition: A small, rapid, rough sketch used to explore multiple layout or design concepts quickly before committing to a direction.

blocks.heading: Industry Standard
blocks.text: Professional designers at companies like Google and Apple routinely fill pages with thumbnail sketches before opening any digital tool. Do not skip this step because you think your first idea is good — the best designs usually emerge from comparison, not inspiration.
Technique 2: Annotated Sketches and Diagrams

An annotated sketch is a more refined drawing with written notes (annotations) that explain design decisions. Annotations clarify things that the drawing cannot: colour choices, font sizes, interactive behaviour, content type, or the reasoning behind a layout decision.

blocks.definition: A hand-drawn or digital design diagram with written notes explaining design decisions, colour choices, content, and functionality.

items: Label all major elements: navigation, hero image, body text, call-to-action button.
Note colours using hex codes, colour names, or references to a brand guide.
Explain why a design decision was made: 'Navigation at top — standard web convention; users expect it here.'
For interactive elements, note what happens on click or hover.
Annotations can be handwritten or typed and added digitally to a scanned sketch.

blocks.text: Technique 3: Wireframes

A wireframe is a structural blueprint of a screen or page. It shows the layout and hierarchy of content — what goes where — without any visual styling (colour, fonts, final images). Wireframes use boxes for images, lines for text, and simple shapes to represent UI components.

blocks.definition: A low-fidelity structural layout of a screen or page that shows content placement and hierarchy without visual design styling.

items.label: Low fidelity (Lo-fi)
points: Hand-drawn or simple digital boxes
No colour or real content
Used in early planning stages
Fast to produce; easy to change
Good for testing layout concepts

items.label: High fidelity (Hi-fi)
points: Digital tool (Figma, Adobe XD, Balsamiq)
Actual colours, fonts, images, and content
Used for client presentations and developer handoff
Takes longer; reflects near-final design
Can be made interactive (clickable prototype)

apps.name: Figma
apps.note: Industry standard for UI/UX design. Browser-based with real-time collaboration. Used at Google, Microsoft, Airbnb. Excellent for both wireframing and hi-fi prototypes.

apps.name: Balsamiq
apps.note: Specialist wireframing tool with a 'sketchy' aesthetic that keeps focus on structure. Very fast for lo-fi wireframes. Popular in agile teams.

apps.name: Adobe XD
apps.note: Part of the Adobe Creative Cloud. Strong prototyping features and integration with Photoshop and Illustrator.

apps.name: Canva
apps.note: Accessible for beginners. Not a specialist wireframing tool but useful for simple layout mockups and poster/presentation design planning.

blocks.text: Technique 4: Storyboards

A storyboard is a sequence of frames that shows how a product will unfold over time — used for video, animation, interactive media, and user journeys through websites or apps. Each frame represents a moment, screen, or scene, with annotations describing the content, transitions, and timing.

blocks.definition: A sequence of annotated frames that represents the flow of a video, animation, interactive experience, or user journey through a digital product.

rows: Sketch or image of what is seen on screen or in frame
What is heard — music, narration, sound effects
How the scene changes — cut, fade, dissolve, swipe
Titles, captions, subtitles, or UI labels
What happens — button press, animation, camera move

blocks.title: Create a Wireframe for a Client Landing Page
blocks.software: Figma (free) or hand-drawn on A4 paper
blocks.intro: Using the music festival brief from Lesson 1, create a lo-fi wireframe for the festival's landing page. Your wireframe must clearly show content hierarchy and all major page sections.
steps: Start with 3 thumbnail sketches exploring different layouts for the landing page. Consider: Where is the event name? Where is the ticket link? What is the hero visual?
Choose your strongest thumbnail and develop it into a labelled wireframe on A4 (or in Figma).
Include these sections in your wireframe: navigation bar, hero section (event name, date, location), lineup preview, ticket purchase call-to-action, footer with social links.
Use boxes with an 'X' through them to represent images. Use horizontal lines to represent text blocks. Label each element.
Add annotations explaining at least four design decisions. For example: 'Ticket button placed above the fold — users should not need to scroll to find it.'
If using Figma: create your wireframe using only rectangles, lines, and text. Do not add colours or final fonts yet.
Present your wireframe to a partner. Ask: Can they identify where to buy tickets without you explaining? Record their feedback.

blocks.title: Create a Storyboard for a Promotional Video
blocks.software: Hand-drawn, Canva, or PowerPoint
blocks.intro: The music festival client wants a 30-second promotional video for Instagram. Create a storyboard planning the full video sequence.
steps: Plan the video structure: Introduction (0–5s), Key information (5–20s), Call to action (20–30s).
Create at least 8 storyboard frames covering the full 30 seconds.
For each frame, sketch the visual, note the audio/music, write any on-screen text, and note the transition to the next frame.
Include at least one text overlay (event name, date), one transition effect (fade or cut), and one call to action ('Get tickets at...').
Annotate two frames in detail, explaining why you chose that visual approach for that moment in the video.
Review your storyboard: does the sequence make sense without sound? If not, revise it.

question.text: What is the key difference between a wireframe and a high-fidelity mockup?
options: A wireframe is used for video projects; a mockup is used for web projects.
A wireframe shows structure without visual styling; a mockup includes colours, fonts, and near-final design.
A wireframe must be hand-drawn; a mockup must be created in a digital tool.
A wireframe is submitted to the client; a mockup is only for the developer.
question.explanation: A wireframe focuses on layout and content hierarchy without any visual design styling. A high-fidelity mockup adds colour, typography, imagery, and detailed styling to show what the final product will look like.

question.text: A student creates a detailed, colourful hi-fi mockup as their first design step, without producing any thumbnail sketches or lo-fi wireframes. Identify TWO problems with this approach and explain how the missing steps would have helped.

blocks.text: Target Audience and Presentation Medium

The most technically perfect product fails if it is designed for the wrong person or delivered in the wrong format. Understanding your target audience and choosing the right presentation medium are core skills in the design process — and are explicitly required in SCSA AIT Unit 3 project work.
Target Audience Analysis

Your target audience is the specific group of people who will use your product. Analysing the audience means going beyond 'young people' or 'adults' to understand their digital habits, preferences, technical ability, context of use, and what they actually need from the product.

items.definition: The specific group of people for whom a digital product is designed, defined by characteristics such as age, digital literacy, interests, and context of use.
A fictional but research-based profile of a typical user, used to guide design decisions and keep the team focused on user needs.
The environment and circumstances in which a user will interact with the product — e.g., on a phone while commuting, or at a desk in a quiet office.

blocks.text: Target Audience Characteristics
When describing your target audience in project documentation, address the following characteristics:

rows: Are they children, teens, adults, seniors?
Affects font size, language complexity, visual style, and assumed knowledge.
Are they confident tech users or occasional users?
Low literacy → simpler navigation, more guidance, fewer features. High literacy → richer functionality acceptable.
Desktop, mobile, tablet? iOS, Android, Windows?
Determines responsive design requirements and file format choices.
Where and when will they use it? Work, home, commute?
Affects content density, attention span assumptions, and accessibility needs.
What are they trying to accomplish?
Ensures design supports the user's task, not just the client's preferences.
Do any users have visual, motor, or cognitive considerations?
May require colour contrast compliance, large text, keyboard navigation, or simple language.

blocks.text: Creating a User Persona

A user persona is a one-page profile of a fictional but representative user. It keeps design decisions grounded in real human needs. Industry teams refer to personas throughout the entire project: 'Would Sarah find this confusing?' is a more productive design question than 'Does this look good?'

blocks.heading: Career Path: UX Designer / Project Coordinator
blocks.text: User personas are a foundational tool in UX design. Senior UX Designers at companies like Spotify, Canva, and the ABC conduct interviews and surveys to build research-backed personas. In your AIT project, your persona can be based on your own experience with the target audience — e.g., if designing for Year 9 students, think about what you knew and needed at that age.
Choosing the Right Presentation Medium

The presentation medium is the format in which the final product will be delivered and consumed. It is not just 'a website' or 'a video' — it includes the platform, the device, the context, and sometimes the software environment. The medium must match the audience's context and the product's purpose.

rows: Any audience with internet access; scalable; accessible on multiple devices
Promotional material, event awareness, community engagement
Audiences already active on the platform; short attention span; mobile-first
Office/academic audiences; needs to be readable offline or printable
Audience being presented to live or watching a recorded walkthrough
Audiences who prefer visual/audio content; commuters; social media users
Audiences who may not be online; physical location contexts
Tech-confident users; gamified or education contexts

blocks.heading: Project Manager Tip
blocks.text: When justifying your choice of medium in project documentation, don't just name it — explain why it suits this specific audience. Example: 'I chose Instagram Stories because the target audience of 18–25 year olds are active Instagram users who access content on mobile; Stories format is optimised for mobile screens and has high engagement rates with this demographic.'

blocks.title: Audience and Medium Match
blocks.scenario: Three clients each need a digital product. Client A: A retirement village wants to help residents aged 65–80 find the weekly activities schedule. Client B: A surf school wants to promote their summer holiday camps to parents of children aged 10–14. Client C: A tech startup wants to onboard new employees with a company policy guide that staff can reference on the job.

questions.question: For each client, identify the most appropriate presentation medium and justify your choice by referencing specific target audience characteristics.
For Client A, describe THREE specific design decisions (not just medium choice) that would make the product accessible and usable for the described audience.
Create a brief user persona for one of the three clients. Include: name, age, digital habits, context of use, primary goal, and one frustration with technology.

blocks.title: Write a Target Audience Analysis
blocks.software: Microsoft Word / Google Docs
blocks.intro: Write a one-page target audience analysis for your current project (or the music festival brief). This document will be part of your project folio.
steps: Write a paragraph describing the target audience using at least four characteristics from the table above (age, digital literacy, device, context, goals, accessibility).

Create a user persona using this structure: Name, Age, Occupation/Year level, Devices they use, Digital confidence (1–5), How they will access your product, Their primary goal, One frustration or barrier.
Write one paragraph justifying your choice of presentation medium, explicitly connecting it to the audience characteristics you identified.
List three design decisions you will make specifically because of this audience (e.g., 'I will use 18pt minimum body font because many users are over 65').
Peer-check: swap your analysis with a classmate. Can they identify who the audience is from your description alone? Revise based on their feedback.

question.text: A student is designing a resource for Year 7 students to learn about online safety. Which medium would LEAST suit this audience and context?
options: An animated explainer video on YouTube
A printed A3 poster for the classroom wall
A 40-page technical PDF report
An interactive quiz website
question.explanation: A 40-page technical PDF report is poorly suited for Year 7 students — the format, length, and likely language complexity do not match the age, attention span, or reading level of the target audience. Animated video, posters, and interactive quizzes are all more appropriate for this age group.

question.text: Define 'context of use' and explain how it should influence design decisions for a digital product. Give a specific example.

blocks.text: Evaluation and Iteration

In professional IT, a product is never truly 'finished' after one pass. Evaluation and iteration — the cycle of testing, gathering feedback, and improving — are what separate professional-quality work from a first attempt. The SCSA AIT assessment requires you to demonstrate this cycle explicitly.

blocks.heading: Industry Standard
blocks.text: Software teams at companies like Atlassian, Canva, and Atlassian run 'sprint reviews' at the end of every two-week development cycle — presenting work, gathering feedback, and adjusting the next sprint accordingly. This is the Agile methodology, and it is built on the principle that iteration produces better outcomes than trying to get everything right the first time.
What Are Evaluation Criteria?

Evaluation criteria are specific, measurable statements that define what a successful product looks like. They are written during the planning phase — before you build anything — so that evaluation at the end is objective, not subjective.

items.definition: Specific, measurable standards against which a completed digital product or solution is assessed. Defined at the planning stage.
Criteria related to what the product does — whether required features work correctly.
Criteria related to visual design — whether design principles are applied effectively and consistently.
Criteria related to user experience — whether the product is easy and intuitive to use for the target audience.
The process of making targeted improvements to a product based on evaluation findings, then re-evaluating.

blocks.text: Writing Strong Evaluation Criteria
Weak criteria are vague and unmeasurable. Strong criteria are specific, testable, and tied to the brief. Compare the examples below:

headers: Why the strong version is better
rows: The website uses a consistent colour palette of no more than three colours that align with the festival brand guide.
Specific, testable, and tied to a design principle (repetition/consistency).
The video is easy to understand.
A person with no prior knowledge of the festival can identify the event name, date, and ticket URL after watching the video once.
Can be tested with a real user; gives a clear pass/fail outcome.
All body text on the poster meets WCAG AA contrast ratio requirements (minimum 4.5:1 for normal text).
References an industry standard; can be verified with a contrast-checking tool.
The budget spreadsheet automatically calculates totals and highlights cells that exceed the budget in red.
Specific functionality; testable with sample data.

blocks.text: Methods of Evaluation

rows: Assess each criterion as met / partially met / not met, with evidence.
Structured, objective overview of how well the product meets the brief.
Present the product to the client; gather feedback using specific questions.
Whether the product meets the client's intent — often reveals mismatches between your interpretation and their vision.
A classmate evaluates the product against the criteria — they do not know your design decisions.
Fresh perspective; identifies blind spots and usability issues you cannot see in your own work.
A member of the target audience uses the product while you observe — without guidance from you.
Reveals real usability issues; where users get confused, what they miss, what they find intuitive.
Honest personal assessment of the process as well as the product.
Demonstrates critical thinking; identifies what you would do differently next time.

blocks.text: The Iteration Cycle

Iteration is not about fixing everything — it is about making targeted improvements based on evidence, then re-evaluating. A single iteration cycle: evaluate → identify the most critical issues → make specific changes → re-evaluate against the same criteria.

items: Evaluate the product against your criteria — document the results clearly.
Prioritise: which issues most significantly affect the product's ability to meet the brief?
Make targeted improvements — document what you changed and why.
Re-evaluate the specific criteria that were not met — did the change work?
Document the iteration: before/after screenshots, change log, and reflection on the outcome.

blocks.heading: Project Manager Tip
blocks.text: In your evaluation write-up, always provide evidence. Screenshots, contrast ratio checker results, user testing notes, and client email responses are all valid evidence. 'I think it meets the criterion' is an opinion. 'The user completed the task in 30 seconds without any help' is evidence.
Portfolio Presentation

In SCSA AIT, your project folio is the complete record of your project management process. It demonstrates that you did not just build a product — you planned, represented, evaluated, and iterated. A strong folio tells a story from brief to final product.

rows: Your restatement of the project requirements in your own words, including target audience and constraints.
Detailed description of your audience with design implications.
Thumbnail sketches, annotated wireframes or storyboards, with notes explaining decisions.
Your planning timeline with milestones and resource allocation.
Your pre-defined criteria — written before you built the product.
Criteria checklist with evidence; peer review feedback; client feedback.
Documentation of changes made based on evaluation, with before/after evidence.
Honest assessment of the process, what you learned, and what you would do differently.

blocks.title: Evaluating a Peer's Product
blocks.scenario: Your classmate has created a website homepage for a local bakery. The original brief required: (1) the bakery's name and opening hours visible without scrolling, (2) at least one high-quality food photo, (3) a contact form, (4) colour palette matching the bakery's existing brand (brown, cream, and terracotta), (5) usable on mobile. You are conducting a peer review.

questions.question: Write a peer review using a criteria checklist format. For each criterion, state whether it is met, partially met, or not met, and provide a specific observation as evidence for your judgement.
Based on your review, identify the TWO most critical issues to fix first and explain why these should be prioritised over other issues.
Write a constructive peer feedback comment (3–4 sentences) that would help the designer improve their product without being vague or discouraging.

blocks.title: Evaluate and Iterate Your Design Representation
blocks.software: Microsoft Word / Google Docs (for documentation)
blocks.intro: Using the wireframe or storyboard you created in Lesson 3, conduct a structured evaluation and complete one iteration cycle.
steps: Write your evaluation criteria: create a checklist of 5–6 specific criteria your design representation must meet (e.g., 'All page sections are labelled', 'The ticket call-to-action is visible above the fold', 'Annotations explain at least 4 design decisions').
Self-evaluate: go through each criterion and rate it met / partially met / not met. Write one sentence of evidence for each rating.
Peer review: swap your wireframe or storyboard with a partner. They complete the same criteria checklist independently. Compare your results — where do you disagree?
Identify the two most important improvements based on the combined self and peer review.
Make the improvements to your wireframe or storyboard. Document each change: what you changed, why you changed it, and which criterion it addresses.
Photograph or export your revised design representation and annotate it to show what changed.
Write a 150-word reflection: What did the evaluation process reveal that you had not noticed yourself? What would you do differently in the planning phase next time?

question.text: Which evaluation criterion is written most effectively?
options: The app looks professional and modern.
Users find the app easy to navigate.
A Year 10 student can find the homework submission page within 30 seconds without any instruction.
The app has good design principles applied.
question.explanation: Option C is specific (Year 10 student), measurable (30 seconds), and testable (without instruction). The other options are vague, subjective, or cannot be objectively verified — they describe impressions rather than measurable outcomes.

question.text: Why should evaluation criteria be written BEFORE the product is built, not after?
options: Because criteria are not needed once the product is finished.
To ensure the evaluation is objective and not influenced by what was actually built.
Because clients will not accept criteria written after the deadline.
To save time at the end of the project.
question.explanation: Writing criteria after the product is built introduces bias — you unconsciously write criteria that match what you made, rather than what was originally required. Pre-defined criteria ensure the evaluation measures the product against the brief, not against itself.

question.text: A student evaluates their project poster and writes: 'I think it looks good and I am happy with it.' Explain why this is an inadequate evaluation and describe what a professional evaluation response would include instead.

blocks.heading: Project Manager Tip — Final Portfolio Checklist
blocks.text: Before submitting your project folio, check: (1) Does your folio include evidence of ALL phases — plan, represent, create, evaluate, iterate? (2) Have you written an interpreted brief that shows you understood the client's needs? (3) Do your design representations include annotations, not just drawings? (4) Are your evaluation criteria specific and measurable? (5) Do you have evidence (screenshots, feedback, test results) for every criterion judgement? (6) Does your reflection show genuine critical thinking — not just what went well, but what you would do differently?

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #2001 · SW LOGISTICAL ANALYTICS
blocks.text:
Status: 🔴 URGENT | Sprint 1 | Assigned to: Analyst

Leo Thorne flagged it first: his client-facing dashboard is loading in 14 seconds. I checked the data payloads — Leo's UI is requesting 4MB PNG chart exports on every page load. Sparky Miller's network can handle it, but the client's branch office in regional WA is on a 10Mbps ADSL connection and it's timing out. We need to audit every file in this pipeline, understand what's compressed and what isn't, and make deliberate choices — not defaults. File compression is not a design decision. It's an engineering decision.
Why Compression Decisions Are Professional Decisions

Every unoptimised file in a system has a cost: storage space, bandwidth, load time, and infrastructure spend. A 24MB RAW photograph and a 300KB JPEG can look identical on screen, but one costs 80 times more to serve. At SW Logistical Analytics, we do not accept file size defaults — we choose compression deliberately based on what the file is for and who receives it.

items.definition: Encoding data using fewer bits than the original, reducing file size. Can be applied to any file type — documents, images, audio, video, or data archives.
Permanently discards some data to achieve smaller file sizes. The original cannot be exactly reconstructed. Best for media where minor quality loss is acceptable.
Reduces file size by encoding repeated patterns without discarding any data. The original file can be perfectly reconstructed. Essential for data files and professional assets.
The ratio of original file size to compressed file size. A 4:1 ratio means the compressed file is one quarter of the original size.
The progressive quality degradation that occurs when a lossy-compressed file is decompressed, edited, and re-compressed multiple times.
A visible distortion introduced by lossy compression — commonly seen as blurry edges, pixelated blocks, or noise in images and video.

blocks.text: Lossy vs Lossless — The Core Trade-Off

items.label: Lossy Compression
points: Permanently discards data the algorithm judges as less perceptible
Achieves significantly smaller file sizes than lossless
Each re-save degrades quality further — generational loss
Best for: photographs for web, audio for streaming, video for delivery
Never use for: logos, charts, data files, documents, archival masters
Formats: JPEG (image), MP3 (audio), AAC (audio), H.264/MP4 (video)

items.label: Lossless Compression
points: Finds and encodes repeated patterns — no data discarded
Original file perfectly reconstructed on decompression
Larger file sizes than lossy — but full quality is preserved
Best for: logos, charts, data archives, professional editing masters, CSV/JSON
Never compromise: if quality integrity matters, use lossless
Formats: PNG (image), FLAC (audio), WAV (audio, uncompressed), ZIP (any), PDF (document)

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · SLOANE VANE, SW LOGISTICAL ANALYTICS
blocks.text: Leo saves his dashboard charts as JPEG because 'they're smaller.' Every time he re-exports from his design tool, the compression runs again. After six iterations, the axis labels on that bar chart are a blur. Always keep master files in lossless formats. Export lossy only at the final delivery stage — never work in lossy formats.
Real File Size Impact — What the Numbers Look Like

rows: Master file — never shared, never served to web
When exact pixel data must be preserved
Web thumbnails — artefacts visible at full size
Sending large data exports to clients — all data preserved

blocks.text: Calculating Compression Savings
Sloane uses this formula when auditing Leo's dashboard payloads and calculating bandwidth savings for client proposals.

items.definition: ((Original Size − Compressed Size) ÷ Original Size) × 100. Example: ((24 MB − 3 MB) ÷ 24 MB) × 100 = 87.5% saving.

blocks.text: Format Decisions at SW Logistical Analytics

rows: Large photographic backgrounds on web (too large)
Logos and icons at any scale
Web image delivery — better quality at smaller size than JPEG
Systems that don't support modern formats
