Files that are already compressed (adds no benefit)
Final reports for clients — layout preserved
Data that needs to be processed or queried

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · AUDIT LEO'S DASHBOARD
blocks.text:
Leo Thorne's dashboard audit has identified these files in the pipeline. For each one, identify the problem with the current format choice, state the correct format, and calculate the potential file size saving.

1. sw-logo-header.jpg — 280 KB, JPEG at 60% quality. Used as the company logo in the dashboard header at 120×40px.
2. delivery-performance-chart.png — 4.2 MB, PNG. A bar chart with 6 data points, served on every page load.
3. quarterly-briefing-audio.wav — 31 MB, WAV. A 3-minute internal briefing for client distribution.
4. client-data-archive.zip — 45 MB, ZIP containing CSVs. Already compressed. Correct?
5. client-report-final.jpg — 2.8 MB, JPEG of a scanned and signed approval document.

For each: state the problem, correct format, and estimated size after conversion. Then calculate: how much total bandwidth is saved per page load if all issues are corrected?

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · SLOANE IS AUDITING YOUR WORK
blocks.text:
Before I sign off on these compression decisions, answer these:

1. A colleague says converting Leo's 4.2 MB PNG chart to JPEG will reduce it to around 300 KB — a 93% saving. Is this always the right decision? What would be lost, and what would the consequences be for a client reading the chart's axis labels at 150% zoom?

2. Explain generational loss in concrete terms using the JPEG chart example. If Leo exports the chart as JPEG, someone edits it in Photoshop and saves again as JPEG, and this happens four more times — what accumulates?

3. A client wants to archive 10 years of audio briefings. Each MP3 is 7 MB. They ask whether converting to FLAC will save space. What do you tell them — and why is this different from the WAV → MP3 conversion?
Check Your Understanding

question.text: Leo Thorne exports the SW Logistical Analytics company logo as a JPEG at 60% quality to reduce dashboard load time. What is the primary problem with this decision?
options: JPEG files cannot be displayed in web browsers
JPEG lossy compression introduces artefacts on sharp edges and text, making the logo look blurry and unprofessional
The file size saving is not significant enough to justify using JPEG
JPEG files cannot be used in client-facing dashboards
question.explanation: JPEG uses lossy compression that introduces visible artefacts — particularly on sharp edges and text. Logos have sharp edges, flat colours, and fine text that JPEG compression degrades visibly. PNG (lossless) or SVG (vector) are always the correct choice for logos.

question.text: A 24 MB RAW photograph is compressed to 4 MB as a JPEG. What is the file size saving percentage?
question.explanation: Saving % = ((24 − 4) ÷ 24) × 100 = (20 ÷ 24) × 100 = 83.3%. The compressed file is 83.3% smaller than the original.

question.text: Compressing a ZIP archive that already contains JPEG images will significantly reduce the total file size.
question.explanation: False. JPEG images are already compressed using lossy compression. Applying ZIP (lossless) compression to already-compressed files yields little to no additional size reduction because the data no longer contains the repeated patterns that lossless compression exploits. ZIP is most effective on uncompressed files like TXT, CSV, and DOCX.

question.text: SW Logistical Analytics archives audio briefings from client meetings for future legal reference. Which format is most appropriate for these archives?
options: MP3 at 128 kbps — smallest file size
AAC at 256 kbps — good balance of quality and size
FLAC — lossless compression preserves full audio quality for future playback and legal purposes
MP4 — widely compatible video format
question.explanation: FLAC is lossless — the compressed file can be perfectly decompressed to recreate the original audio. For legal archives, data integrity is non-negotiable. MP3 and AAC are lossy and permanently discard audio data. MP4 is a video container format.

question.text: Explain what generational loss is and why Sloane's rule — 'never work in lossy formats, only export lossy for final delivery' — prevents it. Give a specific example from the SW Logistical Analytics context.
question.explanation: Generational loss is the cumulative quality degradation that occurs when a lossy-compressed file is decompressed, modified, and re-compressed multiple times. Each save cycle applies lossy compression again, discarding more data than the last. Example: if a data visualisation chart is exported as JPEG, edited to update figures, saved as JPEG again, then adjusted for colour and saved again as JPEG — each round of saves degrades the chart's axis labels and line edges further. By the third or fourth save, text may be unreadable and data points ambiguous. Sloane's rule prevents this by maintaining the master chart in a lossless format (PNG or the original tool's native format) and only exporting a JPEG copy at the point of client delivery — never for working files.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #2002 · SW LOGISTICAL ANALYTICS
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 HIGH | Sprint 1 | Assigned to: Analyst

Leo Thorne designed a new client report this week. The same photograph appears in: (a) a printed A4 executive summary, (b) the web dashboard banner, and (c) an email newsletter thumbnail. He exported one file — a 24 MB TIFF — and sent it everywhere. The printed version looks sharp. The web version takes 8 seconds to load. The email client rejected it as over-size. Three destinations, three completely different technical requirements. You need to know this before you touch any file destined for client delivery.
One Source, Multiple Destinations

The single most important rule in file optimisation: always start with the highest-resolution source file and produce separate, purpose-built exports for each destination. You can never recover detail from a file that was exported too small. You can always produce a smaller version from a larger one.

items.definition: A measure of print resolution — how many ink dots are placed per inch. Higher DPI = sharper printed output. Professional print requires 300 DPI.
A measure of digital image resolution — how many pixels appear per inch on screen. Standard screens use 72–96 PPI.
Changing the number of pixels in an image. Downsampling (reducing pixels) shrinks file size. Upsampling (adding pixels) reduces quality — it invents data that was never there.
Removing pixels from the edges of an image. Changes aspect ratio, removes irrelevant content, and reduces file size.
The colour system an image uses. RGB is used for all digital screens. CMYK is used for professional ink printing. Sending a CMYK image to a website causes colour inaccuracy.
The width-to-height proportion of an image or display area. Common ratios: 16:9 (widescreen), 1:1 (square/Instagram), 9:16 (portrait/mobile stories).

blocks.text: Platform-Specific Requirements

rows: Maximum 600 px wide — email clients often block large images

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · SLOANE VANE, SW LOGISTICAL ANALYTICS
blocks.text: Leo's mistake was a single export for multiple destinations. The professional workflow is: one master file, multiple exports named by destination. SWL-ClientLogo_print_300dpi.pdf, SWL-ClientLogo_web_72ppi.png, SWL-ClientLogo_email_600px.jpg. Name the export file by its destination — not by the date or your initials. The destination is what matters.
Two Techniques for Reducing File Size Before Exporting
Cropping

Cropping removes pixels from the edges of an image. A 4032 × 3024 pixel phone photo served as a 600px-wide email thumbnail is still 4032 pixels wide internally — the browser just scales it down on the fly, wasting bandwidth. Crop to the exact required dimensions before exporting. Every pixel you don't send is bandwidth you don't consume.
Resampling (Downsampling)

Resampling adjusts the pixel count. Downsampling a 300 DPI print image to 72 PPI for web use reduces the pixel dimensions and file size dramatically while maintaining visual quality at screen size. Most image editors use algorithms (Bicubic, Lanczos) that preserve sharpness when reducing. Never upsample — enlarging a small image does not add detail; it blurs.

items.definition: Pixels = Physical Size (inches) × DPI. Example: A4 at 300 DPI = 8.27 × 300 = 2481 px wide. Same A4 at 72 PPI = 8.27 × 72 = 595 px wide — a dramatically smaller file.

blocks.text: Professional Export Workflow

items: Always start with the highest-resolution source: camera RAW, layered PSD, or SVG vector
List all destinations: print, web dashboard, email, social media
For each destination: identify the required dimensions, DPI/PPI, colour mode, and format
Crop to the required aspect ratio for each destination
Downsample to the required pixel dimensions — never upsample
Convert colour mode if required (RGB for screens, CMYK for professional print)
Export with appropriate compression settings for the format
Name each export file by destination: filename_web_1920px.jpg, filename_print_300dpi.pdf
Test: view the web export in a browser; check the print export in preview at 100%

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · THREE PLATFORM EXPORTS
blocks.text:
SW Logistical Analytics has a new client photograph: 4032 × 3024 px at 72 PPI, saved as JPEG at 90% quality — approximately 5.2 MB. The photograph needs to be used in three places:

1. Printed A4 executive report (300 DPI, professional printer, CMYK)
2. SW Logistical Analytics website news banner (1920 × 600 px, web, RGB)
3. LinkedIn post (1200 × 628 px, social media, RGB)

For each export:
- State the required pixel dimensions
- State the colour mode and format
- Describe the cropping or resampling steps
- Calculate the pixel dimension formula for the print export (A4 width at 300 DPI)
- Estimate the output file size relative to the 5.2 MB source

Also: write three export filenames following Sloane's naming convention (include destination in the name).

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · SLOANE IS AUDITING YOUR WORK
blocks.text:
Before I approve these exports, answer these:

1. The original photograph is 4032 × 3024 px and Leo wants to use it at 600 px wide in an email newsletter. He proposes just setting the HTML width attribute to 600px in the email code. Why is this incorrect — even though it will display at 600px? What should he do instead?

2. A colleague exports the print version using RGB colour mode instead of CMYK. The digital proof looks fine. The printed version has noticeably different colours from the screen version — particularly the reds. Explain why this happened.

3. The client provides a new logo at 200 × 80 px as a PNG. They want it used at 400 × 160 px on the website header. Your colleague upsamples it in Photoshop. What will the result look like and why? What should the client have provided instead?
Check Your Understanding

question.text: SW Logistical Analytics is preparing a brochure for professional printing. What DPI and colour mode should the images be set to?
options: 72 PPI, RGB
300 DPI, CMYK
96 PPI, RGB
150 DPI, CMYK
question.explanation: Professional print requires 300 DPI for sharp output and CMYK colour mode — the colour system used by inkjet and offset printers. 72 PPI is for screen display. Sending RGB files to a professional printer results in colour inaccuracies.

question.text: An image for SW Logistical Analytics' website dashboard needs to be A4 width at 72 PPI. Using the formula Pixels = Size (inches) × PPI, what is the pixel width? (A4 width = 8.27 inches)
options: 595 pixels
2481 pixels
1920 pixels
300 pixels
question.explanation: 8.27 inches × 72 PPI = 595.44 pixels — approximately 595 pixels. Compare this to A4 at 300 DPI = 8.27 × 300 = 2481 pixels. The web version is approximately one quarter the pixel width of the print version — and the file size difference is even more dramatic.

question.text: Upsampling an image — increasing its pixel dimensions beyond the original — improves its quality for high-resolution printing.
question.explanation: False. Upsampling adds pixels by interpolation — the software estimates values for new pixels. This does not add real detail that was never captured. The result is a larger file that appears blurry or soft when printed. Always use the highest-resolution original available.

question.text: A client submits a photograph at 4032 × 3024 px (approximately 5.5 MB) for use as a 600 px-wide email newsletter banner. Describe two specific optimisation steps that should be applied and explain the benefit of each.
question.explanation: 1. Cropping: The original 4:3 aspect ratio photograph should be cropped to match the banner's proportions (approximately 3:1 for a wide banner). This removes irrelevant image area, improves composition, and directly reduces the pixel count and file size before any resampling. 2. Resampling (downsampling): Reduce pixel dimensions from 4032 × 3024 to 600 × 200 px (or similar banner dimensions). This dramatically reduces file size — from approximately 5.5 MB to well under 100 KB — meaning the email loads quickly and is not rejected by email clients that enforce file size limits. A JPEG export at 80–85% quality after downsampling is appropriate.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #2003 · SW LOGISTICAL ANALYTICS
blocks.text:
Status: 🔴 URGENT | Sprint 2 | Assigned to: Analyst

The client has requested an audit trail for the Q2 delivery schema — they need to know exactly what changed between the version their system integrated in May and the version that caused a data mismatch in June. We have four files in the folder: schema_v2.csv, schema_v2_edited.csv, schema_FINAL.csv, and schema_USE_THIS.csv. I can't determine which file was approved, when each change was made, or who authorised it. This is a data governance failure. Version control is not a workflow preference — it is a professional requirement with legal implications.
The Problem Version Control Solves

Without version control, every file-based project eventually becomes a chaos of confusingly named files, overwritten work, and lost audit trails. In a data analytics environment, the consequences are more severe than in a document-only workflow: a schema change that cannot be traced can corrupt a client's database, trigger SLA breaches, and expose the firm to liability.

items.definition: A system for tracking and managing changes to files over time, enabling specific versions to be retrieved, compared, or restored.
A record of all changes made to a document or file, including author, timestamp, and a description of what changed.
A storage location for files and their complete change history — used in formal version control systems like Git.
A saved snapshot of changes in a version control system, with a message describing what was changed and why.
Reverting a file or project to a previous version — essential when a change introduces errors or breaks a dependent system.
A document or file that records what changed between each version of a file — who changed it, when, and why.

blocks.text: Professional File Naming — The SW Logistical Analytics Standard

rows: Major = breaking change; minor = small correction
Chronological sorting in any file browser

blocks.text: A complete filename: SWL-CoastalFreight_DataSchema_v2.1_20250701_SV_approved.csv. Anyone on the team — or an auditor — can determine the project, content, revision, date, author, and approval status without opening the file.
Professional Folder Structure for Version Control

items: 01_Briefs — client requirements, contracts, scope of work
02_Working — versioned active drafts; only analysts with write access
03_Review — files at review stage sent to client or team lead
04_Approved — only client-approved versions; write-protected where possible
05_Assets — source data files (CSV, JSON), raw imagery, reference materials
06_Delivery — final files as delivered; what the client actually received and when

blocks.text: Cloud Built-In Version History — Your Safety Net

items.label: OneDrive / SharePoint
points: Right-click any file → Version History to see and restore any previous version
SharePoint adds check-in/check-out: prevents two people editing simultaneously
Versions retained for 30+ days (enterprise plans: 180 days)
No manual action required — saves automatically on every change

items.label: Google Docs / Drive
points: File → Version History → See Version History shows all changes with timestamps and authors
Named versions can be set at key milestones (e.g. 'Client-Approved-v2.0')
Shared Drives allow team-wide access without individual account dependency
History is retained indefinitely for Google Workspace accounts

blocks.text: Git — The Industry-Standard System for Code and Data

Git is the world's most widely used formal version control system. Originally designed for software code, it is now used for data pipelines, SQL queries, configuration files, and any text-based file where change tracking and team collaboration are critical. At Y12 level, you need to understand Git conceptually and know how it applies in a data analytics context.

rows: A folder where Git tracks every change to every file
The SWL-CoastalFreight project folder in OneDrive with full version history
A saved checkpoint with a description of what changed and why
Saving v2.1 with a changelog entry: 'Added TrackingID column per client request 2025-07-01'
A parallel copy of the project for testing changes without affecting the main version
A test copy of the schema to trial a new column structure before client approval
Bringing a branch's changes back into the main version after testing
Moving approved test schema changes into the live client file
Download (pull) or upload (push) changes from a shared server
Syncing your OneDrive files — downloading team changes, uploading your edits
Cloud hosting for Git repositories — used for team collaboration
The equivalent of a shared OneDrive for Git-tracked files

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · SLOANE VANE, SW LOGISTICAL ANALYTICS
blocks.text: Every IT job advertisement I've reviewed in the last five years lists Git as a required skill. Even if you're not writing code, you'll be expected to version-control SQL queries, data schemas, and configuration files. Understanding Git at a conceptual level — what a commit is, what a branch does, why you don't edit the main branch directly — separates candidates who've worked in professional environments from those who haven't.

blocks.title: Build a Professional Version Control System
blocks.software: Windows File Explorer or OneDrive, Word or Google Docs
blocks.intro: You will set up a complete professional document management system for a mock client project and apply version control practices.
steps: Create a project folder called SWL-MockClient_Project and inside it create the six numbered subfolders: 01_Briefs, 02_Working, 03_Review, 04_Approved, 05_Assets, 06_Delivery.
Create three documents in 02_Working using the correct naming convention: a DataSchema CSV, a DeliveryReport DOCX, and a ClientBrief DOCX. Version each as v1.0 with today's date in YYYYMMDD format.
Make a substantive change to the DataSchema document and save it as v1.1 — do not overwrite v1.0.
Write a changelog entry (4–5 sentences) describing what changed between v1.0 and v1.1 and why.
Move the v1.1 DataSchema to 03_Review to simulate sending for approval. Record the date in the changelog.
Simulate approval: copy it to 04_Approved with status changed to 'approved' in the filename.
Take annotated screenshots of your complete folder structure and at least two file history views (if using OneDrive or Google Drive).
Write a one-page reflection: why is this system more professional than saving everything to the desktop with names like 'schema_edited_FINAL.csv'?

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · GIT CONCEPTUAL AUDIT
blocks.text:
A client's data team uses Git to version-control their data pipeline scripts. They've provided their commit history:

Commit 4f2a9d3 — 'updates' — 2025-06-28 — Alice
Commit 7c81b2e — 'fixed bug' — 2025-06-27 — Bob
Commit 3a55f91 — 'changed stuff' — 2025-06-25 — Alice
Commit 9d04c78 — 'Added TrackingID column to delivery schema, updated import script to match — resolves Ticket #1847' — 2025-06-20 — Sloane

Identify three problems with Alice and Bob's commit messages. Then rewrite commit 4f2a9d3 as a professional commit message, assuming the change was adding a new 'DestinationPort' column to the delivery schema.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · SLOANE IS AUDITING YOUR WORK
blocks.text:
Before I accept this document management system, answer these:

1. A colleague argues that OneDrive's automatic version history makes manual version numbering in filenames redundant. Give two specific reasons why manual version control through filenames is still necessary even when automatic version history is enabled.

2. The folder structure uses numbered prefixes (01_, 02_). Why does this matter — what specific problem does it prevent that alphabetical naming doesn't?

3. You have a data schema in 04_Approved. A client requests a significant structural change — three columns removed, two added. Walk through every step of your version control process, including what version number the new file gets and what happens in each folder.
Check Your Understanding

question.text: A client requests a minor correction to an approved data schema file (SWL-CoastalFreight_DataSchema_v2.0_20250615_approved.csv). What is the correct version number for the corrected file?
options: v2.0 — overwrite the existing approved file
v2.1 — minor increment for a small correction
v3.0 — new major version for any client request
v2.0-edit — append a suffix to indicate the edit
question.explanation: Minor corrections increment the minor version number — from v2.0 to v2.1. The original v2.0 file must never be overwritten. A new major version (v3.0) would be appropriate for a significant structural change. Appended suffixes like '-edit' are not a recognised convention and create ambiguity.

question.text: Which date format ensures files sort in correct chronological order when listed alphabetically in File Explorer?
options: DDMMYYYY — e.g. 01072025
MMDDYYYY — e.g. 07012025
YYYYMMDD — e.g. 20250701
question.explanation: YYYYMMDD (ISO 8601 standard) sorts files correctly alphabetically because the most significant part (year) comes first. DDMMYYYY causes January files from 2024 and 2025 to appear together before February files. YYYYMMDD is the only format that sorts correctly without any configuration.

question.text: In Git terminology, explain what a 'commit' is and why writing a descriptive commit message (rather than 'updates' or 'fixed stuff') is considered professional best practice in a data analytics team.
question.explanation: A commit is a saved snapshot of all changes made to files in a Git repository at a specific point in time. It records what was changed, when, and who made the change. A descriptive commit message (e.g. 'Added TrackingID column to delivery schema — resolves Ticket #1847') allows team members and auditors to understand what changed without comparing files line by line. In a data analytics context, this is critical when clients request audit trails, when debugging a data mismatch, or when rolling back to a known good version. Vague messages like 'updates' provide no actionable information and are unprofessional in a team environment.

question.text: SW Logistical Analytics is onboarding a new client who currently stores all their data files on a shared network drive with no version control — files are overwritten directly and named without conventions. Propose a complete document management system for this client. Your answer should cover: recommended platform, folder structure, file naming convention, version control approach, and at least two specific risks the system addresses. (6 marks)
question.explanation: A strong answer covers: Platform: recommend OneDrive for Business or Google Workspace Shared Drive — justifying with automatic version history, access control, and multi-device access. Folder structure: at minimum 01_Briefs through 06_Delivery with numbered prefixes — explain why numbered prefixes avoid alphabetical sorting issues. File naming: ProjectCode_DocumentType_vX.X_YYYYMMDD_Status.ext — explain each element. Version control: never overwrite; create new versions; use major.minor numbering; maintain a changelog. Two risks addressed: (1) Accidental overwrite — version history and new-version protocol means previous versions are always recoverable. (2) Audit trail gaps — descriptive filenames and changelogs mean any version change can be traced to a person, date, and reason. Additional risks may include: no clear current version identification (solved by folder structure with 04_Approved), difficulty working remotely (solved by cloud platform).

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #2004 · SW LOGISTICAL ANALYTICS
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 HIGH | Sprint 2 | Assigned to: Analyst

A prospective client — a Perth-based medical logistics firm — is asking whether their client records should move to cloud storage. Their data includes patient transport records. There are two competing voices in the room: their CFO wants to cut IT infrastructure costs immediately, and their compliance officer is asking about data sovereignty and the Australian Privacy Act. Your job is to give them a balanced, informed recommendation — not a sales pitch for whichever provider we have a referral relationship with.
What is Cloud Computing?

Cloud computing is the delivery of computing resources — servers, storage, databases, networking, software, and analytics — over the internet from remote data centres. Instead of purchasing and maintaining physical hardware, organisations access these resources on demand and pay only for what they use.

items.definition: The delivery of computing services (servers, storage, software, databases) over the internet, accessed on demand from remote data centres.
Software delivered via the internet and accessed through a browser, with no local installation required. Examples: Google Workspace, Microsoft 365, Xero, Canva.
Cloud-based computing infrastructure — virtual servers, storage, and networking — rented from a provider. Examples: AWS EC2, Azure Virtual Machines.
A cloud platform for developers to build and deploy applications without managing underlying infrastructure. Examples: Google App Engine, Azure App Service.
The ability to increase or decrease computing resources instantly to match demand, without purchasing physical hardware.
Storing data across multiple physical locations so that if one server or data centre fails, data remains available from another.
The principle that data is subject to the laws of the country where it is physically stored. Data stored on US servers is subject to US laws, regardless of the data owner's nationality.

blocks.text: Cloud Service Models

items.label: SaaS — Software as a Service
points: Ready-to-use applications accessed via browser or app
No infrastructure to manage — provider handles everything
Examples: Google Workspace, Microsoft 365, Xero, Salesforce, Canva
Best for: small–medium businesses, standard workflows, fast deployment

items.label: IaaS — Infrastructure as a Service
points: Virtual servers and storage rented from provider
Organisation manages the operating system and software
Examples: AWS EC2, Azure VMs, Google Compute Engine
Best for: businesses needing custom configurations or large-scale computing

items.label: PaaS — Platform as a Service
points: Development environment in the cloud — build apps without managing servers
Provider manages infrastructure; developer manages the application
Examples: Google App Engine, Heroku, Azure App Service
Best for: software development teams building web or mobile applications

blocks.text: Advantages of Cloud Computing

items: Accessibility — access files and applications from any device, anywhere, with an internet connection
Cost efficiency — no upfront hardware costs; pay-as-you-go eliminates capital expenditure
Automatic backups — providers replicate data across multiple locations, protecting against hardware failure
Scalability — increase or decrease storage and computing power instantly without purchasing hardware
Collaboration — multiple team members access and edit the same files simultaneously in real time
Automatic software updates — providers handle maintenance, reducing IT overhead
Disaster recovery — data in the cloud survives local fires, theft, floods, and hardware failures
Sparky Miller's note: cloud platforms reduce demand on local network infrastructure — less data is stored and processed on-premises

blocks.text: Risks and Disadvantages

items: Internet dependency — no internet access means no cloud access; critical for businesses in areas with unreliable connectivity
Data sovereignty — data stored overseas may be subject to foreign laws, including law enforcement access
Vendor lock-in — migrating large datasets from one provider to another is costly and technically complex
Ongoing subscription costs — over time, subscription fees may exceed the cost of owned hardware
Security and privacy — a data breach at the cloud provider could expose sensitive client data
Service outages — when a provider experiences downtime, all cloud-dependent services become unavailable

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · SLOANE VANE, SW LOGISTICAL ANALYTICS
blocks.text: Data sovereignty is not a theoretical concern in Australia. The Privacy Act 1988, the Australian Privacy Principles, and sector-specific regulations (health, finance, government) can all impose requirements on where data is physically stored. A medical logistics firm storing patient transport data on a US cloud server — even a reputable one — may be in breach of the Privacy Act without an Australian-region data storage selection. Always check: does this provider offer an Australian data centre region? Can we select it explicitly in the configuration?
Major Cloud Providers — Australian Context

rows: Largest cloud platform globally — common in enterprise and government
Azure VMs, Azure SQL, OneDrive, Microsoft 365
Dominant in Australian business and government — integrates with Microsoft tools
Strong in data analytics and machine learning workloads
File storage, version history, SharePoint integration
Most widely used in Australian schools and SMEs
Widely used in education and SMEs

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · RECOMMEND FOR THE MEDICAL LOGISTICS FIRM
blocks.text:
SW Logistical Analytics needs to recommend a cloud storage solution for a Perth-based medical logistics firm (25 staff). They handle patient transport records, schedule data, and billing. Requirements:
- Data must be physically stored in Australia
- Must support file version history (client has had data loss from overwrites)
- Must be accessible on mobile devices in the field
- Budget: under $25 AUD per user per month
- The client currently uses Microsoft Outlook for email

Research OneDrive for Business (Plan 2), Google Workspace Business Standard, and AWS S3 with an Australian region. For each:
1. Confirm whether Australian-region storage is available and how it's configured
2. Find the price per user per month (current as of today)
3. Identify version history features
4. Note mobile app support

Create a comparison table, then write a 150-word recommendation with specific reference to data sovereignty and the client's requirement for version history.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · SLOANE IS AUDITING YOUR WORK
blocks.text:
Before I present this to the medical logistics client, answer these:

1. The CFO says 'our data is backed up by Microsoft — we don't need local backups anymore.' What would you tell them? Does cloud storage eliminate the 3-2-1 backup requirement, and if not, why not?

2. The compliance officer asks: 'If Microsoft or Amazon receives a legal subpoena from US authorities, can they access our patient data stored in their Australian data centres?' What is the answer, and what does this mean for the firm's risk profile?

3. The firm's field teams are in areas of Western Australia with unreliable mobile data. How does this affect the cloud-only recommendation, and what mitigation would you propose?
Check Your Understanding

question.text: SW Logistical Analytics recommends Microsoft 365 Business Standard to a client. Which cloud service model does this represent?
options: IaaS — the client is renting virtual server infrastructure
PaaS — the client is building custom applications on a cloud platform
SaaS — the client is accessing ready-made software applications via the internet
On-premises — Microsoft 365 is installed on local hardware
question.explanation: Microsoft 365 is SaaS (Software as a Service) — ready-to-use applications (Word, Excel, Teams, Outlook) delivered over the internet with no local installation of the core service required. The client pays a subscription and accesses software through a browser or thin client.

question.text: A client's patient transport data is stored in the AWS Sydney region. Under what legal framework is this data primarily governed?
options: US law only — because AWS is an American company
Australian law — because the data is physically stored in Australia
International law — because cloud data has no national jurisdiction
No law — cloud data is exempt from national regulations
question.explanation: Data sovereignty means data is primarily governed by the laws of the country where it is physically stored. Data in AWS Sydney is stored in Australia and is primarily subject to Australian law, including the Privacy Act 1988 and Australian Privacy Principles. However, the US CLOUD Act may still allow US authorities to request data from US-based companies operating Australian data centres — this nuance is important in compliance discussions.

question.text: If a business stores all its data in OneDrive for Business with automatic backup enabled, the 3-2-1 backup rule is fully satisfied.
question.explanation: False. OneDrive provides cloud redundancy (data replicated across multiple data centres) and version history, but it is a single service from a single provider. The 3-2-1 rule requires 3 copies on 2 different media types with 1 off-site. If the OneDrive account is compromised, deleted, or the service experiences an outage, a business with no alternative copy has no fallback. At minimum, a secondary backup to a different platform or local storage is recommended.

question.text: An Australian medical logistics firm is evaluating AWS (US-based company) versus Microsoft Azure for storing patient transport records. Identify two specific data sovereignty concerns they should raise and explain why storing data in an Australian-region data centre partially but not completely addresses these concerns.
question.explanation: Concern 1: Foreign law enforcement access — the US CLOUD Act (2018) enables US authorities to compel US-based companies to provide data stored anywhere in the world, including Australian data centres, without notification to Australian parties. Storing data in AWS Sydney reduces exposure but does not eliminate it entirely because AWS remains a US company. Concern 2: Australian Privacy Act compliance — the Privacy Act and Australian Privacy Principles require health data to be handled with strict protections. While storing in an Australian region helps meet the data residency expectation, the firm still needs to verify that the provider's data processing terms, employee access controls, and breach notification procedures comply with Australian health data regulations. An Australian-region selection addresses where data physically sits, but not who can legally compel access to it.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #2005 · SW LOGISTICAL ANALYTICS
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 HIGH | Sprint 3 | Assigned to: Analyst

Sparky Miller flagged our analytics workstation last week — it's running a scheduled Python data job at 35% of its normal speed and Disk Clean-Up shows 23 GB of recoverable space. Antivirus definitions haven't updated in six days. The last full scan was three weeks ago. This machine processes client data daily and it's running like it was bought in 2015. System maintenance is not an IT department problem for someone else to solve — every analyst at this firm owns the state of the machine they work on.
Why System Maintenance is a Data Integrity Issue

A poorly maintained system is a liability. Accumulated temporary files consume storage and slow processing. Fragmented disk data increases read times. Outdated antivirus definitions leave client data exposed. At SW Logistical Analytics, where we process sensitive logistics and financial data, a compromised or degraded system is a contractual and reputational risk — not just a performance inconvenience.

items.definition: A Windows utility that identifies and safely removes unnecessary files: temporary files, cached Windows updates, Recycle Bin contents, thumbnail cache, and error log files.
Files created by the OS and applications during normal operation, intended to be short-lived but frequently accumulating to consume significant storage over time.
Local copies of web pages, images, and scripts stored by the browser to speed up repeat visits. Can consume multiple gigabytes of storage.
A condition where file data is stored in non-contiguous locations on a hard disk drive (HDD), requiring the read head to move between multiple positions to read a single file — slowing performance.
Reorganising fragmented file data on an HDD so that file fragments are stored contiguously, improving read speed. Not applicable to SSDs.
Malicious software designed to damage, disrupt, or gain unauthorised access to a system. Includes viruses, ransomware, spyware, trojans, and worms.
Malware that secretly monitors user activity and transmits data — including credentials, browsing history, and files — to unauthorised third parties.
Updates to the threat database used by antivirus and anti-malware software. Without current definitions, new threats are undetectable.

blocks.text: Disk Clean-Up and Temporary File Management

Windows accumulates junk automatically: update installation files kept post-install, browser cache, Recycle Bin contents, thumbnail indexes, application temp files, and system error logs. On a machine that hasn't been cleaned in six months, Disk Clean-Up run as Administrator can routinely recover 10–25 GB — storage that would otherwise slow down write operations and fill analytics working directories.

items: Windows Update files — often 5–15 GB alone; kept 'just in case' rollback is needed after installation
Browser cache — Chrome and Edge can accumulate 1–5 GB of cached web data
Recycle Bin — deleted files occupy storage until the bin is emptied
Thumbnail cache — Windows Explorer image previews; rebuilds automatically when needed
Temp folder (C:\Users\[User]\AppData\Local\Temp) — accumulated application working files

blocks.text: Disk Fragmentation — HDD vs SSD

items.label: HDD (Hard Disk Drive)
points: Stores data on spinning magnetic platters
A physical read/write head moves to the location of data
Fragmented files require the head to move to multiple positions — slower
Defragmentation reorganises fragments onto contiguous sectors
Monthly defragmentation recommended (Windows does this automatically)

items.label: SSD (Solid State Drive)
points: Stores data in flash memory chips — no moving parts
Access speed is uniform regardless of where data is stored
Fragmentation does not affect SSD performance
Running a defragmenter on an SSD causes unnecessary write cycles, wearing it out faster
Windows uses TRIM for SSDs instead — automatically manages flash cell usage

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · SLOANE VANE, SW LOGISTICAL ANALYTICS
blocks.text: Sparky Miller checked the analytics workstation — it's running an SSD. The analyst who defragmented it last month wasted an hour and shortened the drive's lifespan. Windows 10 and 11 detect drive type automatically and apply the correct maintenance. If it's an SSD, let Windows manage it with TRIM. If you're not sure what storage type a machine has, check Device Manager before touching anything.
Anti-Malware, Antivirus, and Threat Defence

Modern threat landscapes go far beyond viruses. At SW Logistical Analytics, the risk is not a crashed machine — it's undetected spyware exfiltrating client logistics data for months before anyone notices. Multi-layered protection is the minimum standard.

items: Antivirus — detects known threats using a signature database; requires current definition updates
Anti-malware — broader detection covering ransomware, spyware, adware, and zero-day exploits
Real-time protection — continuously monitors file and network activity, blocking threats as they occur
Ransomware — encrypts files and demands payment; can render an entire client data archive inaccessible
Spyware — runs silently, capturing credentials, screen content, or keystrokes; often undetected for weeks
Keyloggers — a category of spyware recording every keystroke, capturing passwords and client data inputs

blocks.text: Spam Filters — Email as the Primary Attack Vector

The majority of cyberattacks on Australian businesses arrive via email — phishing links, malicious attachments, and business email compromise (BEC) scams. Spam filters are not just for blocking advertising; they are the first line of defence against the most common attack vector in corporate environments. Microsoft Defender for Office 365 and similar enterprise email security gateways scan every inbound and outbound message for malicious content.
System Maintenance Schedule

rows: Remove temp files, update remnants, Recycle Bin, cache
Monthly, or when storage is low
Reorganise fragmented data for faster access
Weekly full scan; real-time always on
Check junk folder; update filter rules
Free storage; fix browser performance; remove cached sensitive data

blocks.title: Run a System Maintenance Audit
blocks.software: Windows (Disk Clean-Up, Windows Security, Settings, Device Manager)
blocks.intro: Perform and document a real system maintenance check on a Windows computer — the analytics workstation in your scenario or your own school computer.
steps: Open Disk Clean-Up: search 'Disk Clean-Up' in Windows, select C: drive. Record what categories are available and the total recoverable space.
Run as Administrator: click 'Clean up system files' and note how much additional space is available. Record the difference between standard and admin mode.
Open Windows Security → Virus and Threat Protection. Record: last scan date, last definition update, and whether real-time protection is active. Screenshot this screen.
Open Device Manager → Disk Drives. Identify whether the primary storage device is an HDD or SSD. Take a screenshot.
Open Settings → System → Storage. Record what is consuming the most space on the C: drive.
Open your browser → Settings → Privacy and Security → Clear Browsing Data. Note the estimated cache size before clearing.
Create a maintenance report table: Task | Tool Used | Finding | Recommended Action.
Write a one-page summary for a non-technical manager: what is the current health state of this system, what risks exist, and what maintenance should be scheduled?

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · MAINTENANCE PLAN FOR THE ANALYTICS WORKSTATION
blocks.text:
SW Logistical Analytics' analytics workstation hasn't been maintained in 6 months. Sparky Miller's assessment report states:
- C: drive is 89% full (only 22 GB free of 200 GB)
- Last antivirus scan: 3 weeks ago
- Antivirus definitions: 6 days out of date
- Last Disk Clean-Up: not recorded
- Drive type: SSD (Samsung 870 EVO)
- A new 'analytics_helper.exe' process appeared in Task Manager last Tuesday — not a known application

Your tasks:
1. Prioritise the maintenance issues by risk level (Critical / High / Medium) with justification.
2. Write a maintenance action plan: for each issue, specify the tool, the exact steps, and the expected outcome.
3. Address the unknown 'analytics_helper.exe' process — what does this potentially indicate and what do you do?
4. Since this is an SSD: what maintenance tasks from the standard schedule do NOT apply, and why?

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · SLOANE IS AUDITING YOUR WORK
blocks.text:
Before I approve your maintenance plan, answer these:

1. You've identified that antivirus definitions are 6 days out of date. How many new malware variants could have been released in that time, and why does this create a specific risk for the client data we process on this machine?

2. The C: drive is 89% full. Beyond performance issues, what specific risk does critically low disk space pose to an analytics workstation that runs scheduled Python data jobs daily?

3. A colleague suggests the unknown 'analytics_helper.exe' is probably just a Windows update. You're not sure. Walk me through exactly how you would investigate this — what tools, what specifically you check, and at what point you escalate to Sparky Miller.
Check Your Understanding

question.text: The SW Logistical Analytics analytics workstation runs an SSD. A colleague runs Disk Defragmenter to try to improve its performance. What is the outcome?
options: Performance improves because file fragments are reorganised into contiguous sectors
No change in performance — SSDs are not affected by fragmentation; however, the unnecessary write cycles reduce the SSD's lifespan
The SSD is wiped — defragmenter deletes data when run on flash storage
Windows prevents defragmenter from running on SSDs entirely
question.explanation: SSDs access data electronically with no moving parts — fragmentation has no impact on SSD read speed. Running a defragmenter on an SSD writes data unnecessarily, consuming write endurance cycles and shortening the drive's lifespan. Windows 10 and 11 automatically apply TRIM to SSDs instead of defragmentation.

question.text: Antivirus definitions on the analytics workstation are 6 days out of date. What is the primary risk?
options: The antivirus software will stop working and need to be reinstalled
New malware variants released in the last 6 days are not in the threat database and cannot be detected
The operating system will become unstable without current definitions
Definition updates are only cosmetic — they don't affect threat detection
question.explanation: Antivirus software identifies threats by matching files against a database of known signatures (definitions). New malware is created and released constantly. Any threat released in the 6 days since the last update is not in the database — the antivirus has no knowledge of it and cannot detect or block it.

question.text: Spam filters only block unwanted advertising emails and play no role in protecting against cyberattacks.
question.explanation: False. Email is the most common vector for cyberattacks on Australian businesses. Spam filters scan for phishing links, malicious attachments, spoofed sender addresses, and business email compromise (BEC) attacks. Blocking these at the email gateway prevents many attacks before they reach users.

question.text: An unknown executable 'analytics_helper.exe' has appeared in the Task Manager on SW Logistical Analytics' analytics workstation. Describe the investigation steps you would take to determine whether this is a legitimate application or a potential threat, and identify which type of malware this might indicate.
question.explanation: Investigation steps: 1. Right-click 'analytics_helper.exe' in Task Manager → Open File Location to identify where on disk the executable is stored. Legitimate applications are typically in C:\Program Files or C:\Windows\System32 — unknown locations (e.g. AppData\Temp or Downloads) are suspicious. 2. Search the filename in a reputable threat database (e.g. VirusTotal.com — upload or hash-check the file). 3. Check Windows Event Viewer for the process start time and user account that launched it. 4. Review network activity: use Resource Monitor or a tool like Wireshark to check whether the process is making unexpected outbound connections. If any of these steps indicate suspicious behaviour (non-standard location, unknown network connections, no legitimate software matches the name), escalate to Sparky Miller immediately and isolate the machine from the network. This behaviour pattern — a silently running executable making network connections — is consistent with spyware or a Trojan.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #2006 · SW LOGISTICAL ANALYTICS
blocks.text:
Status: 🟢 SPRINT PROJECT | Sprint 4 | Assigned to: Analyst

New client onboarding: Vasco Logistics, a mid-size freight brokerage in Fremantle. They've handed us a USB drive containing: raw photos from their warehouse (uncompressed RAW, no naming convention), a client database dump in CSV (2019 vintage, no version), a promotional video (uncompressed MOV, 4.2 GB), and three versions of their service agreement document with names like 'contract_FINAL_REAL_v2.docx'. Their IT setup is a shared network drive with no cloud backup and an antivirus that last updated in February. This is your project. Own it.
Project Overview

This project integrates all skills from the Managing Data topic. You will take a disorganised client asset collection, apply professional data management practices across compression, file optimisation, version control, cloud strategy, and system maintenance, and deliver a complete professional recommendation package.
Task 1 — File Compression Audit and Decisions

blocks.title: Task 1: Compression Audit
blocks.intro: Audit the Vasco Logistics file collection and make explicit compression decisions for each asset type.
steps: List all file types in the Vasco USB collection (RAW photos, CSV, MOV video, DOCX documents). For each, state the current format and whether it is compressed (lossy, lossless, or none).

The promotional video is a 4.2 GB MOV. Identify the target format and compression type for: (a) delivery to Vasco for their website, (b) archiving the master copy. State the expected file size range for each and justify the compression choice.

The warehouse RAW photos average 24 MB each. Export format decisions needed for: (a) the SW Logistical Analytics printed client report (A4, 300 DPI), (b) the Vasco Logistics website gallery, (c) the Vasco LinkedIn business page. State format, compression type, and expected file size for each.
Calculate the file size saving percentage for: converting a 24 MB RAW photo to a JPEG for web at 4 MB; converting the 4.2 GB MOV to H.264 MP4 at an estimated 420 MB.
Write a compression policy statement (half page) for Vasco Logistics: when should they use lossy vs lossless, and what master file rule should they always follow?

blocks.text: Task 2 — Multi-Platform File Optimisation

blocks.title: Task 2: Platform-Specific Exports
blocks.intro: Select one Vasco Logistics warehouse photograph (use a royalty-free substitute from Unsplash.com if needed). Produce three platform-specific exports.
steps: Record the original image: pixel dimensions, file size, format.

Export 1 — Print (A4 executive report): 300 DPI, CMYK, TIFF or JPEG at 95% quality. Use the formula Pixels = Size (inches) × DPI to calculate the required pixel width for A4. Record the output dimensions and file size.
Export 2 — Website hero banner: 1920 × 600 px, 72 PPI, RGB, JPEG at 80% quality. Crop first to the correct aspect ratio, then downsample. Record output file size.
Export 3 — LinkedIn post: 1200 × 628 px, 72 PPI, RGB, JPEG at 85% quality. Record output file size.
Build a comparison table: Destination | Pixel Dimensions | DPI/PPI | Colour Mode | Format | File Size | Saving % vs Original.
Name each export file using Sloane's naming convention — include the client code 'VL', destination, and correct date format.
Write one paragraph explaining why a single high-resolution export for all three destinations would cause problems — be specific about file size, load time, and colour mode.

blocks.text: Task 3 — Version Control System

blocks.title: Task 3: Version Control for Vasco Data Files
blocks.intro: Vasco has three versions of their service agreement: 'contract_FINAL_REAL_v2.docx', 'contract_new.docx', and 'contract_USE_THIS_ONE.docx'. Apply professional version control.
steps: Identify four naming convention violations in the existing file names.
Rename all three files using the SW Logistical Analytics convention. Assign version numbers (v1.0, v1.1, v2.0) in a logical order — you decide which represents the most recent approved version.
Design the folder structure (01–06) and specify which folder each renamed file belongs in.
Write a changelog document for the service agreement — invent plausible changes between each version (3–4 sentences per version change).
The CSV client database is from 2019 with no version number. Rename it with the appropriate convention and explain what version number system you would apply going forward.
Write a 200-word version control policy for Vasco Logistics covering: naming convention rules, version numbering approach, folder structure, and the rule about never overwriting approved files.

blocks.text: Task 4 — Cloud Strategy Recommendation

blocks.title: Task 4: Cloud Storage for Vasco Logistics
blocks.intro: Vasco currently uses only a shared network drive. They have 12 staff, handle some sensitive client freight contracts, and have a modest IT budget.
steps: Research two cloud storage options appropriate for a 12-person logistics SME: compare OneDrive for Business Plan 2 and Google Workspace Business Standard.
For each: find current Australian pricing per user per month, storage allocation, version history retention, Australian data centre availability, and mobile app support.
Evaluate data sovereignty: does Vasco's client freight contract data trigger any Australian privacy regulation concerns? Research the Australian Privacy Principles (APP 8 — Cross-border disclosure).
Create a comparison table: Provider | Price/User/Month | Storage | Version History | AUS Data Centre | Mobile App | Key Risk.
Write a 200-word recommendation specifying which provider you recommend and why, with explicit reference to data sovereignty, version control features, and cost.

blocks.text: Task 5 — System Maintenance Plan

blocks.title: Task 5: Maintenance Schedule for Vasco IT
blocks.intro: Vasco's office computer runs Windows 11 on an SSD. Antivirus last updated February. No maintenance records exist.
steps: Identify five critical maintenance tasks for this machine, ordered by priority (address the most severe risk first).
For each task: name the tool, describe the steps in plain language, state the frequency, and explain what risk it mitigates.
Specifically address: should Vasco run Disk Defragmenter on their SSD? Justify your answer technically.

Vasco processes client payment details for freight invoices. Write a paragraph explaining why current antivirus definitions are especially critical in this context — reference spyware and keyloggers specifically.
Format the complete maintenance plan as a professional schedule table that a non-technical office manager can follow.

blocks.text: Task 6 — Integration Analysis

blocks.title: Integration Analysis — Vasco Logistics
blocks.scenario: You have completed the Vasco Logistics data management review. Reflect on how these professional practices interconnect.

questions.question: Explain how a poor file compression decision (e.g. using lossy JPEG for the client CSV data export) could directly impact Sparky Miller's network integration of Vasco's data pipeline. What would happen to the data integrity? (3 marks)

Vasco sends Leo Thorne the 4.2 GB MOV promotional video to embed on their website. Leo embeds it directly without compression. Sparky Miller's analysis shows the Vasco website is now serving 4.2 GB to every visitor. Calculate the bandwidth cost if 500 visitors access the page in one day. If H.264 MP4 compression reduces the file to 420 MB, what is the new daily bandwidth consumption? (3 marks)

Vasco's 2019 CSV database is integrated into their new logistics platform. Six months later, a column structure change is required. Without version control, what specific risks arise? Describe three risks and their potential business consequence. (4 marks)

The compliance officer at Vasco asks whether moving their client freight contracts to Google Drive satisfies their obligations under the Australian Privacy Principles. Provide a balanced answer — what does cloud storage with an Australian data centre address, and what does it not address? (4 marks)

blocks.text: Project Submission Checklist

items: Task 1 — Compression audit table (all Vasco file types, format, compression type, target format)
Task 1 — File size saving calculations for RAW→JPEG and MOV→MP4 conversions
Task 1 — Compression policy statement for Vasco Logistics (half page)
Task 2 — Three platform exports with comparison table (dimensions, DPI, colour mode, format, file size, saving %)
Task 2 — All exports named using Sloane's convention (client code, destination, date, format)
Task 3 — Four naming convention violations identified with corrections
Task 3 — All three service agreement files renamed and placed in correct folder structure
Task 3 — Changelog document for service agreement versions
Task 3 — Version control policy (200 words)
Task 4 — Cloud provider comparison table
Task 4 — Cloud recommendation (200 words) referencing data sovereignty and APP 8
Task 5 — Maintenance schedule table (5 tasks, tool, frequency, risk addressed)
Task 5 — Written justification on defragmentation and SSD
Task 5 — Paragraph on antivirus relevance for payment data processing
Task 6 — Written responses to all four integration analysis questions

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · SLOANE VANE, SW LOGISTICAL ANALYTICS
blocks.text: Everything in this project — compression, optimisation, version control, cloud strategy, system maintenance — is a decision with a professional consequence. The file format choice affects Sparky's pipeline. The version control failure creates a legal exposure. The cloud recommendation involves someone's regulatory obligations. Data management is not admin work. It is the foundation everything else is built on.
Check Your Understanding

question.text: Vasco Logistics wants to archive their client contract documents in a format that preserves layout identically and cannot be easily edited. Which format is correct?
options: DOCX — widely editable
CSV — structured and lightweight
PDF — preserves layout and is not easily editable
PNG — image format visible on any device
question.explanation: PDF (Portable Document Format) preserves document layout across all devices and platforms and is not easily editable. It is the standard format for final client documents, contracts, and reports that must be read consistently regardless of the recipient's software.

question.text: Vasco's 4.2 GB MOV promotional video needs to be embedded on their website. After H.264 MP4 compression it becomes 420 MB. If 500 visitors watch the video in one day, how much total bandwidth is consumed?
options: 420 MB total
4.2 GB total
210 GB total
2.1 TB total
question.explanation: 420 MB × 500 visitors = 210,000 MB = 210 GB per day. Compare this to the uncompressed version: 4.2 GB × 500 = 2,100 GB (2.1 TB) per day. Compression reduces daily bandwidth consumption by 90%, which directly reduces hosting costs and improves page load performance for visitors.

question.text: A company that stores client data using Google Drive's Australian data centre region is fully compliant with all Australian Privacy Principles and has no further privacy obligations.
question.explanation: False. Storing data in an Australian data centre region addresses the physical location requirement but does not satisfy all Australian Privacy Principles. Obligations remain around: how data is handled and who can access it (APP 11), cross-border disclosure rules even within a multinational provider's infrastructure (APP 8), and the provider's security practices, breach notification obligations, and employee access policies. Compliance requires review of the full provider agreement, not just data centre location selection.

question.text: Vasco Logistics' logistics database CSV has been live and integrated for 6 months with no version control. A column name change is now required. Identify three specific risks of making this change directly to the existing file without version control, and for each risk describe a real consequence for Vasco's operations.
question.explanation: Risk 1: No rollback capability — if the column name change breaks the logistics platform integration, there is no clean previous version to restore. Consequence: the platform data pipeline fails and Vasco cannot process freight orders until the issue is manually diagnosed and corrected — potentially hours of operational downtime. Risk 2: No audit trail — without version numbering and a changelog, it is impossible to determine who made the change, when, or why. Consequence: if a data discrepancy is discovered in a client's shipment record, Vasco cannot demonstrate what the data looked like before the change, creating liability exposure. Risk 3: Downstream system breakage — any system querying the CSV by column name will fail silently or produce errors after the column is renamed. Consequence: Vasco's automated invoicing or dispatch system, which may read the CSV column by name, stops working correctly — incorrect invoices are generated or shipment records are lost.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #2201 · BUNBURY HARDWARE INTEGRATORS
blocks.text:
Status: 🔴 URGENT | Job #BHI-2201 | Assigned to: Junior Technician

First day on the tools with Sparky. A Margaret River winery has called — their network went down at 8am and they've lost access to the point-of-sale system, stock management, and all shared drives. Revenue is stopped. Before Sparky will touch a cable, he needs a written assessment: What type of network do they have? What are the business-critical advantages this network was providing? And what is the real cost to this business of every minute of downtime? Have it on his workbench before the site visit.

blocks.title: Computer Networks: Crash Course CS #28
blocks.caption: Crash Course Computer Science — network types, protocols, and how the internet is structured. Sparky watches this with every new junior on day one.

blocks.text: In Year 11 you learned what a network is and how it works technically. Year 12 asks a harder question: WHY do networks exist, and what does it actually cost a business when they fail? Sparky's approach: 'Every cable I run is a business decision. Understand the business first, then the technology.' Networks are not IT infrastructure — they are business infrastructure.

items.definition: Two or more devices connected to share resources, data, and services. Networks are business infrastructure — their failure is a business failure, not just a technical inconvenience.
A network covering a single building or site. The Margaret River winery operates on a LAN — all devices on the one property sharing a single network infrastructure.
A network spanning large geographic distances — connecting multiple sites. A winery group with three estate properties would use a WAN to connect their sites.

The period when a network is non-functional. Gartner research estimates unplanned downtime costs organisations an average of $5,600 per minute — including lost sales, idle staff, and reputational damage.

rows: Winery: POS, stock management, shared drives, Wi-Fi for tasting room
Total site paralysis — no sales, no stock, no communication
Winery group: HQ connected to three vineyard estates
Inter-site services fail; each site may still function independently on its local LAN
Online wine sales platform, cloud POS, remote staff VPN
Online revenue stops; remote staff locked out; cloud services inaccessible

blocks.heading: The Business Case for Networking
blocks.html:
For the Y12 exam, you need to go beyond listing advantages — you must be able to argue the business case for networking in a specific context. Use this framework:

  1. Resource sharing — shared printers, scanners, and storage reduce hardware costs. In a winery: one high-capacity label printer on the network serves all three departments.
  2. File sharing and collaboration — staff access the same stock database and wine records from any device. No version conflicts, no emailing spreadsheets.
  3. Communication — email, internal VOIP phone calls, video calls with distributors all run through the network.
  4. Centralised management — Sparky can apply software updates, security patches, and access controls from one server. Without this, he'd need to touch every device individually.
  5. Internet access sharing — one NBN connection shared to 30 devices. Without a network, each device needs its own connection — a cost multiple of 30.
  6. Data backup — all sales data and stock records back up automatically to a central server nightly. No network = each staff member responsible for their own backups. Most won't do it.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · THE WINERY DOWNTIME REPORT
blocks.text:
Before the site visit, Sparky needs your written assessment. Answer all three sections:

Section A — Network Classification:
The winery has 1 building, 12 staff PCs, 3 POS terminals, a file server, and wireless access for visitor iPads. Classify the network type. Justify your answer with reference to the specific devices and coverage.

Section B — Business Advantages Being Lost:
List FOUR specific advantages the winery is losing right now during the outage. For each, explain the specific business impact in this context (not a generic advantage).

Section C — Downtime Cost Estimate:
The winery's tasting room processes an average of $2,400 in sales per hour. The winery employs 12 staff at an average of $28/hour. The outage is now at 90 minutes. Calculate the estimated minimum revenue loss and the staff idle-time cost. What is the total minimum cost of this 90-minute outage to the business?

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · SPARKY IS PUSHING BACK
blocks.text:
Sparky read your assessment on the drive to the winery. His comments:

Comment 1: 'You classified it as a LAN. Correct. But the winery's online sales platform is cloud-based and accessed through the internet. Is the internet connection part of the LAN? Where does the LAN end and something else begin? Add a sentence clarifying this.'

Comment 2: 'Section B — you listed "file sharing" as a lost advantage. Good. But you said "staff can't access files." Be specific: which files and what is the business consequence of not accessing THOSE files right now, at 10am on a Saturday in the peak season?'

Comment 3: 'Your cost estimate. The winery also has two staff members who are now on hold with suppliers waiting to confirm a Monday delivery order. How do you quantify that cost? Is your "minimum cost" actually the minimum?'

question.text: A boutique hotel has 45 rooms, a restaurant, a reception desk, and a management office. All are in the same building. What type of network connects all devices in this hotel?
options: WAN — Wide Area Network, because it covers a large building
LAN — Local Area Network, because all devices are on one site
Internet — because the hotel connects to online booking systems
MAN — Metropolitan Area Network, because the hotel is in a city
question.explanation: A LAN covers a single site — regardless of the number of devices or the size of the building. All 45 rooms, the restaurant, reception, and office are on the same property, making this a LAN. The hotel may also connect to the internet or cloud services via this LAN, but the internal network is still a LAN.

question.text: A small accounting firm with 8 staff is considering whether to invest $15,000 in a proper server and wired network, or to continue using a simple Wi-Fi router with each staff member saving files to their own laptop. Using TWO specific business advantages of networking, argue the case for the $15,000 investment.
question.explanation: Strong answers select context-relevant advantages: (1) Centralised data backup — an accounting firm holds critical client financial data. Files on individual laptops are at risk of permanent loss if a laptop fails, is stolen, or is infected with ransomware. A central server enables automated nightly backups of all client files, protecting the firm from data loss that could result in professional negligence claims. (2) Security and access control — accounting firms handle confidential financial records that must be protected under privacy law. A server-based network enables IT to control who accesses which files, log access attempts, and apply password policies centrally. Individual laptops with no central control cannot enforce these protections. (2 marks per advantage: advantage stated + specific business-context justification)

question.text: Explain TWO advantages and ONE disadvantage of networking for the Margaret River winery (12 staff, peak tourist season, online wine sales). In your answer, relate each point specifically to the type of business and its context.
question.explanation: Advantages (context-specific): (1) Shared POS and stock management — a networked POS system allows all three terminals to read from the same real-time inventory database, preventing overselling of limited-edition wines and ensuring staff across the tasting room, cellar door, and dispatch area see the same stock figures simultaneously. This is critical during peak season when multiple sales channels are active concurrently. (2) Centralised backup of sales records — all daily transaction records, customer data, and online order history back up automatically to the server each night. If an individual POS terminal fails, no data is lost and the transaction history is immediately available for accounting and compliance. Disadvantage (context-specific): Security risk — the winery's network holds customer credit card transaction data, which must comply with PCI DSS (Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard). A network attack or data breach could expose this data, resulting in significant financial penalties, legal liability, and reputational damage to a brand built on trust and premium positioning. Award 2 marks per advantage with business-context specificity; 2 marks for a disadvantage with specific risk named and its consequence for this business type.

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · JAXEN 'SPARKY' MILLER, BUNBURY HARDWARE INTEGRATORS
blocks.text: I don't sell cable. I sell uptime. When a business owner calls me at 7am because their network is down, they're not thinking about Cat6 versus Cat6a. They're thinking about their revenue, their staff standing around doing nothing, and the customer who just walked out because the EFTPOS wouldn't work. Understand the business cost before you touch the technology. If you can calculate the downtime cost faster than the business owner can, you've already earned your callout fee.

blocks.heading: 🎟️ TICKET #2208 · BUNBURY HARDWARE INTEGRATORS
blocks.text:
Status: 🟡 HIGH | Job #BHI-2208 | Assigned to: Junior Technician

The winery network is back up — turned out to be a faulty Cat5e patch cable. While we were on site, the owner asked Sparky to quote on upgrading the entire cabling infrastructure. The current network is a mix of old Cat5e, one run of Cat6 to the office, and Wi-Fi everywhere else. The owner wants to run a new cellar automation system that transfers 2GB sensor data files every 15 minutes. Sparky needs a transmission media recommendation with technical justification. That's your job.

Transmission media is the physical or wireless path data travels between devices. Year 12 goes beyond identifying the three types — you need to evaluate them for a specific use case, calculate whether a medium can handle the required data rate, and justify your recommendation with technical evidence. Sparky's rule: 'The cheapest cable that meets the spec is the right cable. But you have to PROVE it meets the spec.'

items.definition: The speed at which data moves across a medium, measured in bits per second (bps). Converting file sizes from bytes to bits: multiply by 8. A 2 GB file = 16 Gb = 16,000 Mb.
The maximum data capacity of a transmission medium under ideal conditions. Real-world throughput is typically 60–80% of theoretical maximum due to overhead, interference, and protocol handling.
The time delay between transmitting and receiving data. Critical for real-time applications (POS transactions, video calls, sensor control systems). Optic fibre has the lowest latency of any medium.
Signal weakening over distance. All physical media experience attenuation — copper degrades faster over distance than optic fibre. This is why Cat6a has a 100m maximum distance limit.

blocks.title: The Three Transmission Media — Technical Comparison

items.label: Optic Fibre
items.description: Light pulses through glass strands. Speeds: up to 100 Gbps+. Distance: kilometres without repeaters. Zero electrical interference. Immune to water damage. Cost: high installation (specialist termination tools, fusion splicing). Best for: inter-building runs, ISP backbone, high-throughput industrial systems.

items.label: Copper Wired (Ethernet)
items.description: Electrical signals through twisted copper pairs. Cat6: up to 10 Gbps at 55m; 1 Gbps at 100m. Cat6a: 10 Gbps at 100m. Susceptible to EMI (motors, fluorescent lighting, industrial equipment). Cost: moderate. Best for: office desktops, fixed workstations, server rooms. Not suitable near high-EMI industrial equipment.

items.label: Wireless (Wi-Fi)
items.description: Radio waves through air. Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax): theoretical 9.6 Gbps; real-world 300–800 Mbps per device. Affected by walls, distance, interference from other devices and appliances. Shared medium — bandwidth divided among all connected devices. Cost: low setup. Best for: mobile devices, visitor access, areas where cabling is impractical.

rows: None — radio waves vulnerable to interference
Variable — shared medium, drops under load

blocks.heading: The Calculation Sparky Always Does First
blocks.html:
Before recommending any medium, Sparky calculates whether it can handle the required throughput. Here's the method:

Winery cellar automation requirement:

  File size: 2 GB = 2 × 8 = 16 Gb = 16,000 Mb
  Transfer frequency: every 15 minutes
  Required throughput: 16,000 Mb ÷ 15 min ÷ 60 sec
                    = 17.8 Mbps sustained

Options:
  Wi-Fi 6 real-world: ~400 Mbps  ✓ (speed OK)
  But: EMI from cellar equipment?  ✗ (reliability risk)
  But: Is Wi-Fi shared with visitors? ✗ (contention risk)

  Cat6a at 10 Gbps:  ✓ (speed OK)
  EMI from cellar motors: RISK — need shielded Cat6a (S/FTP)

  Optic Fibre to cellar: ✓ (overkill on speed, zero EMI risk)
  Cost: higher, but justified by industrial environment

Sparky's recommendation: Optic fibre for cellar run.
Office = Cat6a. Visitor areas = Wi-Fi 6.

blocks.heading: 💻 SANDBOX CHALLENGE · THE TRANSMISSION MEDIA RECOMMENDATION
blocks.text:
Write Sparky's transmission media recommendation for the winery upgrade. Structure it as a professional quote document with three sections:

Section A — Data Rate Analysis:
The cellar automation transfers 500 MB files every 5 minutes AND the office runs daily video calls at 8 Mbps per session (4 concurrent sessions). Calculate the required throughput for each use case. Show your working.

Section B — Medium Recommendation:
For each zone (cellar, office, tasting room visitor Wi-Fi), recommend a specific medium and standard (e.g. Cat6a, optic fibre single-mode, Wi-Fi 6). Justify each recommendation with at least TWO technical criteria from your comparison.

Section C — Trade-Off Analysis:
The owner asks: 'Why not just use Wi-Fi everywhere? It's cheaper to install.' Write a professional response explaining what the owner gains and what they risk by choosing all-wireless, using specific technical details relevant to the winery environment.

blocks.heading: 🛑 PR REVIEW · SPARKY IS PUSHING BACK
blocks.text:
Sparky reviewed your recommendation on his laptop in the van. Comments before the quote goes to the client:

Comment 1: 'Section A — your throughput calculation for the video calls. You added 4 × 8 Mbps = 32 Mbps. But you didn't add headroom. Network engineers always add 20–30% headroom for overhead, retransmission, and protocol handling. Redo the calculation with 25% headroom. Does your recommended medium still handle it?'

Comment 2: 'You recommended Cat6a for the office. Good. But the office is 85m from the server room. Check your spec sheet: does Cat6a at 10 Gbps support 85m? What happens to performance if it doesn't? What is your contingency?'

Comment 3: 'Your Wi-Fi trade-off analysis mentions "interference" but doesn't name the specific interference sources in a winery cellar environment. Name them. The owner needs to understand WHY wired is better in THEIR building, not in a generic office.'

question.text: A file is 750 MB. It needs to transfer in under 60 seconds. What is the minimum required network speed?
options: 750 Mbps
100 Mbps
100 MBps
6,000 Mbps
question.explanation: 750 MB × 8 = 6,000 Mb. 6,000 Mb ÷ 60 seconds = 100 Mbps sustained. Wait — 100 Mbps would take exactly 60 seconds under ideal conditions, but real-world throughput is 60–80% of rated speed. At 80% efficiency, a 100 Mbps link delivers ~80 Mbps real throughput, meaning transfer time = 6,000 ÷ 80 = 75 seconds. To complete in under 60 seconds with headroom, you need approximately 125–150 Mbps minimum. Nearest standard answer: 750 Mbps (a 1 Gbps Ethernet link) provides comfortable headroom. Always add headroom in professional calculations.

question.text: A winery cellar uses large electric motors for temperature control and bottling equipment. Explain why this environment creates a specific risk for copper Ethernet cabling, and identify the most suitable transmission medium with technical justification.
question.explanation: Electric motors generate electromagnetic interference (EMI) — a fluctuating electromagnetic field that induces noise into adjacent copper conductors through a process called electromagnetic induction. Standard Cat6 and even shielded Cat6a copper cable running alongside or near motor power cables can experience data corruption, packet loss, and speed reduction as the copper conductors pick up the EMI. In a winery cellar with multiple industrial motors, this is a significant reliability risk. The most suitable alternative is optic fibre: light signals travelling through glass strands are completely unaffected by electromagnetic fields — light does not interact with magnetic forces. Optic fibre (even multimode OM3/OM4) provides 10 Gbps+ capacity with zero EMI vulnerability, making it the correct choice for any run near industrial electrical equipment. (2 marks for explaining EMI mechanism; 2 marks for recommending optic fibre with technical justification)

blocks.heading: 💡 SENIOR WISDOM · JAXEN 'SPARKY' MILLER, BUNBURY HARDWARE INTEGRATORS
blocks.text: Every technician I've trained gets the speed comparison right. Fibre is fastest, Cat6a is second, Wi-Fi is third. That's the textbook answer and it scores your test mark. What it doesn't tell you is why we ran fibre to the cellar and Cat6a to the office and Wi-Fi only for the visitors — and why the winery owner went home happy instead of calling us back three months later to redo it. The textbook answer is the starting point. The site survey is the answer. Know the theory. Then look at the actual building.
